tier_1_regimens.site: General Soft Tissue Sarcoma (extremity/body wall/head and neck, retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal)
tier_1_regimens.notes: AIM (Doxorubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna) is listed as a preferred neoadjuvant/adjuvant regimen for general soft tissue sarcoma. References 1-4 support its use.

tier_1_regimens.site: General Soft Tissue Sarcoma (extremity/body wall/head and neck, retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Epirubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna is listed as a preferred neoadjuvant/adjuvant regimen. Reference 5.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab with RT followed by adjuvant Pembrolizumab for UPS related sarcomas
tier_1_regimens.site: Extremity/body wall/head and neck (UPS, myxofibrosarcoma, dedifferentiated liposarcoma, pleomorphic liposarcoma)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab with RT followed by adjuvant Pembrolizumab for UPS related sarcomas (limb girdle/extremity UPS/myxofibrosarcoma [MFS]/dedifferentiated liposarcoma [ddLPS]/pleomorphic liposarcoma). Reference 6 (SU2C-SARC032 trial).

tier_1_regimens.site: General Soft Tissue Sarcoma (extremity/body wall/head and neck, retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal) - especially for leiomyosarcoma or if ifosfamide not appropriate
tier_1_regimens.notes: AD (Doxorubicin/Dacarbazine) is listed as Other recommended for neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy, specifically for leiomyosarcoma (LMS) or if ifosfamide is not considered appropriate. References 1,2,11,12.

tier_1_regimens.site: General Soft Tissue Sarcoma (extremity/body wall/head and neck, retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent doxorubicin is listed as Other recommended for neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy. References 1,2,7,8.

tier_1_regimens.site: General Soft Tissue Sarcoma (extremity/body wall/head and neck, retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Ifosfamide/Mesna listed as Useful in certain circumstances for neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy. References 5,8,28.

tier_1_regimens.site: Myxoid liposarcoma (extremity/body wall/head and neck, retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Trabectedin is listed as Useful in certain circumstances for neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy in myxoid liposarcoma. Reference 32.

tier_1_regimens.site: General Soft Tissue Sarcoma (extremity/body wall/head and neck, retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Docetaxel/Gemcitabine listed as Useful in certain circumstances (category 2B) for neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy. References 25,26.

tier_1_regimens.site: General Soft Tissue Sarcoma (all subtypes except liposarcoma? - note: anthracycline-based preferred)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Doxorubicin is a preferred first-line agent for advanced/metastatic STS. References 1,2,7,8.
Epirubicin is a preferred first-line agent. Reference 9.
Liposomal Doxorubicin is a preferred first-line agent. Reference 10.
AD is preferred first-line. References 1,2,11-13.
AIM is preferred first-line. References 1-4,7.
Epirubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna is preferred first-line. Reference 5.
Doxorubicin/Trabectedin is preferred first-line for leiomyosarcoma. References 14,15. This is based on the LMS-04 trial (Pautier et al. Lancet Oncol 2022).

tier_1_regimens.site: NTRK gene fusion-positive sarcomas (regardless of subtype)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Larotrectinib is preferred for NTRK gene fusion-positive sarcomas. Not intended for neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy of nonmetastatic disease. Not recommended for angiosarcoma or pleomorphic RMS. Reference 16 (Drilon et al. N Engl J Med 2018).

tier_1_regimens.site: NTRK gene fusion-positive sarcomas (regardless of subtype)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is preferred for NTRK gene fusion-positive sarcomas. Not intended for adjuvant therapy of nonmetastatic disease. Reference 17 (Demetri et al. ESMO 2018).

tier_1_regimens.site: NTRK gene fusion-positive sarcomas (regardless of subtype)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Repotrectinib is preferred for NTRK gene fusion-positive sarcomas. Reference 18 (Solomon et al. Ann Oncol 2023).
Gemcitabine monotherapy is listed as Other recommended for first-line advanced/metastatic STS.
Docetaxel/Gemcitabine is category 2B for first-line advanced/metastatic. References 25,26.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pazopanib - First-Line Advanced/Metastatic (non-adipocytic, ineligible/not candidate for anthracycline)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Pazopanib is listed as Useful in certain circumstances for first-line therapy in patients ineligible for IV systemic therapy or not candidates for anthracycline-based regimens. For non-adipocytic sarcoma. Reference 19 (Grünwald et al. J Clin Oncol 2020).

tier_1_regimens.site: RET gene fusion-positive tumors (regardless of STS subtype)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Selpercatinib for RET gene fusion-positive tumors. Reference 33 (Subbiah et al. Lancet Oncol 2022).
Dacarbazine/Gemcitabine is category 2B, Useful in certain circumstances for first-line. Reference 27.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Anthracycline-based regimens (as for first-line) - Subsequent Lines
tier_1_regimens.notes: Anthracycline-based regimens as listed for first-line may be considered in subsequent lines with attention to lifetime total doxorubicin dose.
Pazopanib is preferred for subsequent lines in non-lipogenic STS. Recommended for palliative therapy. Reference 19 (van der Graaf et al. Lancet 2012). For non-adipocytic sarcoma.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Eribulin - Subsequent Lines Advanced/Metastatic (liposarcoma category 1, other categories 2A)
tier_1_regimens.evidence_level: Category 1 for liposarcoma, Category 2A for other subtypes

trial_data.trial_name: Phase III trial (Schöffski et al. Lancet 2016)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Eribulin is a preferred subsequent line therapy. Recommended only for palliative therapy. Reference 20,21.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Trabectedin - Subsequent Lines Advanced/Metastatic (liposarcoma and LMS category 1, other category 2A)
tier_1_regimens.site: Liposarcoma and leiomyosarcoma (L-type, category 1), other subtypes (category 2A)
tier_1_regimens.evidence_level: Category 1 for liposarcoma and LMS, Category 2A for other subtypes
tier_1_regimens.notes: Trabectedin is preferred for subsequent lines. Recommended for palliative therapy. References 22-24. Category 1 based on phase III data (Demetri et al. J Clin Oncol 2016).
Docetaxel/Gemcitabine is preferred for subsequent lines. References 25,26.
Dacarbazine as a single agent for subsequent lines. Recommended for palliative therapy. References 22,27.
Ifosfamide/Mesna for subsequent lines. Recommended for palliative therapy. References 5,8,28.
Temozolomide for subsequent lines. Recommended only for palliative therapy. Reference 29.
Vinorelbine for subsequent lines. Recommended only for palliative therapy. Reference 30.
Regorafenib for non-adipocytic sarcoma. Reference 31 (Berry et al. Cancer 2017).
Gemcitabine monotherapy for subsequent lines.
Dacarbazine/Gemcitabine for subsequent lines. Reference 27.
Gemcitabine/Vinorelbine is category 2B for subsequent lines. Reference 34.
Gemcitabine/Pazopanib is category 2B. Reference 35 (Somaiah et al. Cancer 2021).

MFS, UPS, ddLPS, cutaneous angiosarcoma, undifferentiated sarcomas

Cabozantinib is category 2B. Reference 43 (O'Sullivan Coyne et al. Clin Cancer Res 2022).

tier_1_regimens.notes: Afamitresgene Autoleucel for synovial sarcomas only. Reference 44 (D'Angelo et al. Lancet 2024).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Active therapy for progressive, morbid, or symptomatic disease

tier_1_regimens.setting: Active therapy for progressive, morbid, or symptomatic disease

tier_2_handbook.extremity_definition: Soft tissue sarcomas (STS) of the extremity, body wall, and head/neck are a heterogeneous group of malignant mesenchymal tumors arising in the limbs, trunk, and craniofacial regions. They account for the majority of STS, with the extremity being the most common primary site (43%), followed by trunk (10%) and head/neck (9%) [6]. STS are derived from connective tissues including fat, muscle, nerve sheaths, blood vessels, and other mesenchymal structures. More than 50 histologic subtypes are recognized [1]. These tumors have a propensity for hematogenous spread, most commonly to the lungs [MS-2]. The management requires multidisciplinary expertise and adherence to evidence-based guidelines to optimize outcomes [8].
tier_2_handbook.extremity_subtypes: The histologic subtypes of extremity/body wall/head and neck STS are numerous and include adipocytic tumors (e.g., liposarcomas: atypical lipomatous tumor/well-differentiated, dedifferentiated, myxoid, pleomorphic), fibroblastic/myofibroblastic tumors (e.g., myxofibrosarcoma, low-grade fibromyxoid sarcoma, sclerosing epithelioid fibrosarcoma, dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans), so-called fibrohistiocytic tumors (e.g., tenosynovial giant cell tumor, deep fibrous histiocytoma), smooth muscle tumors (leiomyosarcoma), vascular tumors (angiosarcoma, epithelioid hemangioendothelioma), pericytic tumors, chondro-osseous tumors, peripheral nerve sheath tumors (malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor), and tumors of uncertain differentiation (e.g., synovial sarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma, alveolar soft part sarcoma, clear cell sarcoma, extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma, desmoplastic small round cell tumor, PEComa, undifferentiated sarcoma) [ST-1 to ST-3]. The most common subtypes in adults include undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma, liposarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, and synovial sarcoma.
tier_2_handbook.extremity_molecular_pathogenesis: Extremity STS harbor characteristic genetic aberrations. Recurrent chromosomal translocations include SS18::SSX1/SSX2 in synovial sarcoma, FUS::DDIT3 or EWSR1::DDIT3 in myxoid liposarcoma, EWSR1::ATF1/CREB1 in clear cell sarcoma, ASPL::TFE3 in alveolar soft part sarcoma, COL1A1::PDGFB in dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, FUS::CREB3L2 in low-grade fibromyxoid sarcoma, and EWSR1::NR4A3 in extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma [SARC-C 2-4]. Well-differentiated/dedifferentiated liposarcoma shows amplification of 12q13-15 including MDM2 and CDK4. Leiomyosarcoma has complex karyotypes. MPNST is associated with NF1, CDKN2A, and EED/SUZ12 alterations [SARC-C 4]. NTRK-rearranged spindle cell neoplasms are an emerging entity [SARC-C 4]. These markers are identified via FISH, PCR, or NGS; NGS may identify actionable mutations for clinical trials or immunotherapy [SARC-C 1].
tier_2_handbook.extremity_epidemiology: In 2022, an estimated 13,190 new STS cases were diagnosed in the United States with 5,130 deaths [1]. Extremity STS accounts for 43% of all STS, trunk 10%, head and neck 9% [6]. Incidence is underestimated, especially prior to GIST inclusion [MS-2]. Prior radiation therapy is a known risk factor [2-4]. Five-year survival varies by stage and histology; localized disease has better survival than metastatic disease.
tier_2_handbook.extremity_risk_factors: Risk factors include prior therapeutic radiation [2-4], chemical exposures (e.g., herbicides such as Agent Orange) [5], and genetic predisposition syndromes: Li-Fraumeni (germline TP53; STS incidence 12–21% [17-19]), neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1; ~5% develop STS, mostly MPNST [30,31]), hereditary retinoblastoma (RB1; increased risk, especially leiomyosarcoma [11,29]), Lynch syndrome, and familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) [9-13]. Additional factors: older age, but specific quantitative strengths are not provided in the guideline.
tier_2_handbook.retroperitoneal_definition: Retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal soft tissue sarcomas (STS) are malignant mesenchymal tumors arising in the retroperitoneal space or intra-abdominal cavity. They account for approximately 15% of all STS [6]. Common histologies include liposarcoma (well-differentiated/dedifferentiated) and leiomyosarcoma. These tumors often present as large masses and have a propensity for local recurrence and metastasis to liver and peritoneum rather than the lungs [MS-2]. Multidisciplinary management is essential, and neoadjuvant therapy may be considered to facilitate resection [RETSARC-1].
tier_2_handbook.retroperitoneal_subtypes: The main histologic subtypes of retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal STS include liposarcoma (well-differentiated liposarcoma [WDLPS], dedifferentiated liposarcoma [ddLPS]), leiomyosarcoma, and less commonly solitary fibrous tumor, malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor, and undifferentiated sarcoma. WDLPS and ddLPS are the most common, often showing amplification of MDM2 and CDK4 [SARC-C 2]. Leiomyosarcoma arises from smooth muscle, often of vascular origin. Other subtypes include desmoid tumors (covered separately) and gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST, covered in separate guideline).
tier_2_handbook.retroperitoneal_molecular_pathogenesis: Well-differentiated and dedifferentiated liposarcomas share supernumerary ring chromosomes and giant marker chromosomes with amplification of region 12q13-15, including MDM2, CDK4, HMGA2, SAS, and GLI [SARC-C 2]. Leiomyosarcoma has complex genomic alterations without specific recurrent translocations; the guideline previously listed unknown aberrations for leiomyosarcoma but removed that entry in the update [SARC-C 4 deletions]. Other retroperitoneal sarcomas may have specific fusions (e.g., solitary fibrous tumor with NAB2::STAT6 [SARC-C 4]).
tier_2_handbook.retroperitoneal_epidemiology: Retroperitoneal STS accounts for 15% of all STS [6]. The true incidence is not separately reported in the guideline. These tumors are often large at diagnosis and have a high local recurrence rate after surgery. Systemic therapy is considered for high-risk or unresectable disease [RETSARC-2].
tier_2_handbook.retroperitoneal_risk_factors: Risk factors are similar to those for STS in general: prior radiation therapy [2-4], chemical exposures [5], and genetic syndromes such as Li-Fraumeni (TP53), neurofibromatosis, and hereditary retinoblastoma [9-13]. Specific risk factors for retroperitoneal subtypes are not detailed in the guideline.
tier_2_handbook.desmoid_definition: Desmoid tumors, also known as aggressive fibromatoses, are unique mesenchymal neoplasms characterized by well-circumscribed, locally invasive fibroblastic proliferation. They are non-metastasizing but can cause significant morbidity due to local invasion and high recurrence rates [MS-30]. Desmoids are often associated with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) and Gardner syndrome, with CTNNB1 or APC mutations [DESM-1]. They can arise in intra-abdominal, abdominal wall, trunk/extremity, or head/neck locations.
tier_2_handbook.desmoid_subtypes: Desmoid tumors are classified by anatomic location: intra-abdominal, abdominal wall, pelvic, trunk/extremity, head/neck, intrathoracic, and retroperitoneal [DESM-4]. Histologically, they are composed of uniform spindle cells with variable collagen deposition. They are not further subclassified into distinct histologic subtypes, but sporadic and FAP-associated forms exist. Mutations in CTNNB1 (encoding β-catenin) are found in about 85% of sporadic cases, with three hotspots (41A, 45F, 45P) [310].
tier_2_handbook.desmoid_molecular_pathogenesis: Sporadic desmoid tumors frequently harbor activating mutations in CTNNB1 (β-catenin gene) at codons 41 and 45, leading to Wnt pathway activation [310]. Trisomies 8 and 20, and loss of 5q21 (including APC) are also common [SARC-C 3]. In patients with FAP, germline APC mutations predispose to desmoids, often with additional somatic CTNNB1 mutations [310-312]. The CTNNB1 mutation 45F is associated with a higher risk of recurrence [310,313]. Molecular testing via mutation analysis can aid diagnosis [SARC-C 3].
tier_2_handbook.desmoid_epidemiology: Desmoid tumors occur in 7.5% to 16% of patients with FAP [21-24]. The median age at diagnosis in FAP patients is 31 years, with intra-abdominal (53%) and abdominal wall (24%) being the most common locations [24]. Sporadic desmoids are rare; precise incidence is not provided. Spontaneous regression has been reported in up to 20% of patients [DESM-2 footnote e].
tier_2_handbook.desmoid_risk_factors: Risk factors for desmoid tumors include FAP (APC mutation), positive family history of desmoids, prior abdominal surgery, and specific APC mutation sites [24]. For sporadic tumors, CTNNB1 mutations are very common, but the inciting factor is unknown. Female sex and pregnancy are known associations for abdominal wall desmoids, but this is not explicitly stated in the guideline.
tier_2_handbook.pleomorphic_rms_definition: Pleomorphic rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) is a high-grade, aggressive subtype of rhabdomyosarcoma that occurs predominantly in adults. It is distinguished from non-pleomorphic RMS (alveolar, embryonal) by its complex genetic alterations and poor prognosis. According to the NCCN Guidelines, pleomorphic RMS should be treated like high-grade soft tissue sarcoma, using systemic therapy options from the STS algorithm [RMS-1]. It is not to be confused with anaplastic variant in children.
tier_2_handbook.pleomorphic_rms_subtypes: Pleomorphic RMS is a histologic subtype of RMS. The guideline does not further subdivide pleomorphic RMS; it is considered a distinct entity from alveolar, embryonal, and spindle cell/sclerosing RMS [RMS-1]. It is characterized by marked cytologic atypia and pleomorphism.
tier_2_handbook.pleomorphic_rms_molecular_pathogenesis: Pleomorphic RMS has complex genetic alterations without the characteristic PAX3/PAX7 fusions seen in alveolar RMS. The molecular pathogenesis is not detailed in the guideline, but it is noted that pleomorphic RMS should be treated with regimens appropriate for high-grade STS [RMS-1]. The guideline does not provide specific mutation data for this subtype.
tier_2_handbook.pleomorphic_rms_epidemiology: Pleomorphic RMS is the least common RMS subtype and occurs predominantly in adults older than 50 years [374]. The incidence increases with age; in one study of 39 adult patients, pleomorphic histology accounted for 0%, 27%, and 60% in age groups 16–19, 20–49, and 50+, respectively [374]. Among adults with RMS, pleomorphic histology is more common than in children (19% vs 1%) and is associated with unfavorable sites and worse survival (5-year OS 27% in adults vs 63% in children) [372].
tier_2_handbook.pleomorphic_rms_risk_factors: Risk factors for RMS in adults are not well-defined. Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53 mutation) is associated with an increased risk, particularly for RMS in children (55% of STS in TP53 carriers) [17]. Other genetic syndromes may contribute, but specific risk factors for pleomorphic RMS are not mentioned.
tier_2_handbook.non_pleomorphic_rms_definition: Non-pleomorphic rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) includes alveolar, embryonal, and spindle cell/sclerosing subtypes. These occur more frequently in children and adolescents but also affect adults. They are characterized by specific genetic fusions (alveolar: PAX3::FOXO1 or PAX7::FOXO1; spindle cell/sclerosing: VGLL2-related fusions or MYOD1 mutation). Embryonal RMS has complex alterations. Multidisciplinary management involving pediatric expertise is strongly recommended [RMS-1].
tier_2_handbook.non_pleomorphic_rms_subtypes: Non-pleomorphic RMS comprises alveolar RMS (including solid variant), embryonal RMS (including botryoid and spindle cell variants), and spindle cell/sclerosing RMS (with VGLL2-related fusions, intraosseous, or MYOD1 mutation) [RMS-1]. Anaplastic features can be seen in up to 13% of RMS in younger patients [RMS-1 footnote e]. Alveolar RMS is associated with PAX3/PAX7 fusions, while embryonal RMS is typically fusion-negative with complex karyotypes.
tier_2_handbook.non_pleomorphic_rms_molecular_pathogenes: Alveolar RMS harbors recurrent chromosomal translocations: t(2;13)(q35;q14) producing PAX3::FOXO1, t(1;13)(p36;q14) producing PAX7::FOXO1, and t(X;2)(q13;q35) producing PAX3::AFX [SARC-C 2]. Embryonal RMS shows complex alterations involving multiple genes including MYOD1, KRAS, HRAS, TP53, NF1, NRAS, PIK3CA, FBXW7, FGFR4, and BCOR [SARC-C 2]. Spindle cell/sclerosing RMS may have VGLL2-related fusions or MYOD1 mutation, and intraosseous variants may have specific alterations (not detailed). The presence of PAX3::FOXO1 versus PAX7::FOXO1 has prognostic implications in metastatic disease [37].
tier_2_handbook.non_pleomorphic_rms_epidemiology: RMS is the most common STS of children and adolescents, but less common in adults [MS-1]. In adults, non-pleomorphic RMS (alveolar, embryonal) is also seen. The incidence in adults is lower than in children; 5-year OS for adult RMS is approximately 27% compared to 63% for children [372]. Among adult RMS, non-pleomorphic subtypes are less frequent than pleomorphic. The guideline does not provide specific incidence rates for each non-pleomorphic subtype.
tier_2_handbook.non_pleomorphic_rms_risk_factors: Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53 mutation) is a known risk factor for RMS, particularly embryonal and alveolar in children [17]. Other syndromes such as neurofibromatosis and Beckwith-Wiedemann may be associated, but not detailed in this guideline. No specific environmental risk factors are mentioned.
tier_2_handbook.alt_wdlps_definition: Atypical lipomatous tumor (ALT) and well-differentiated liposarcoma (WDLPS) are low-grade adipocytic neoplasms. ALT is the term used for superficial locations (extremity, abdominal wall, trunk), while WDLPS is used for deep-seated tumors (retroperitoneum, paratesticular). Both are characterized by amplification of 12q13-15 including MDM2 and CDK4. They have no metastatic potential unless dedifferentiation occurs. Management involves surgical resection with oncologically appropriate margins; radiation is reserved for selected cases [ALT/WDLPS-1].
tier_2_handbook.alt_wdlps_subtypes: The entity includes ALT (extremity, trunk, abdominal wall) and WDLPS (retroperitoneum, paratesticular, and other deep sites). When dedifferentiation is present, it is classified as dedifferentiated liposarcoma and treated as a high-grade sarcoma [ALT/WDLPS-1]. Histologically, ALT/WDLPS shows adipocytic proliferation with variation in cell size and nuclear atypia, often with fibrous septa and lipoblasts.
tier_2_handbook.alt_wdlps_molecular_pathogenesis: ALT/WDLPS is characterized by supernumerary ring chromosomes and giant marker chromosomes derived from amplification of the 12q13-15 region, leading to overexpression of MDM2, CDK4, HMGA2, SAS, and GLI [SARC-C 2]. This amplification is detectable by FISH or NGS and is a diagnostic hallmark, differentiating ALT/WDLPS from benign lipomas and other liposarcomas. Dedifferentiated liposarcoma maintains the same amplification but with additional genomic complexity.
tier_2_handbook.alt_wdlps_epidemiology: ALT/WDLPS is one of the most common subtypes of liposarcoma. It occurs most frequently in the retroperitoneum and extremities. The guideline does not provide specific incidence numbers, but it is more common than pleomorphic liposarcoma. It has a favorable prognosis unless dedifferentiation occurs.
tier_2_handbook.alt_wdlps_risk_factors: Risk factors for ALT/WDLPS are not specifically identified in the guideline. General STS risk factors such as prior radiation and genetic syndromes may apply, but no strong associations are noted.
tier_2_handbook.borderline_phyllodes_definition: Borderline phyllodes tumor of the breast is a fibroepithelial neoplasm with intermediate malignant potential, characterized by leaf-like stromal growth and variable cellular atypia, mitotic activity, and stromal overgrowth. It is distinct from benign and malignant phyllodes tumors. Management involves surgical excision with negative margins (≥1 cm) and consideration of adjuvant radiation for high-risk features [PHYLLSARC-1].
tier_2_handbook.borderline_phyllodes_subtypes: Phyllodes tumors are classified as benign, borderline, or malignant based on histologic features including stromal cellularity, atypia, mitotic rate, stromal overgrowth, and margin status. Borderline tumors have intermediate features, such as moderate atypia, mitotic count of 5–9 per 10 high-power fields, and pushing margins [PHYLLSARC-1]. The guideline does not further subdivide borderline phyllodes.
tier_2_handbook.borderline_phyllodes_molecular_pathogene: The molecular pathogenesis of borderline phyllodes tumors is not detailed in the NCCN guideline. Genetic counseling and testing are recommended for breast and sarcoma genes plus other inherited cancer genes consistent with family phenotype [PHYLLSARC-1 footnote a]. Specific alterations are not provided.
tier_2_handbook.borderline_phyllodes_epidemiology: Phyllodes tumors are rare, representing less than 1% of breast neoplasms. The incidence of borderline phyllodes specifically is not given in the guideline. They occur in women of all ages, with a peak in the 40s. The guideline does not provide numerical data.
tier_2_handbook.borderline_phyllodes_risk_factors: No specific risk factors for borderline phyllodes tumors are mentioned in the guideline. Genetic predisposition (e.g., Li-Fraumeni) may be considered based on family history [PHYLLSARC-1].
tier_2_handbook.malignant_phyllodes_definition: Malignant phyllodes tumor of the breast is a rare, aggressive fibroepithelial neoplasm characterized by overtly malignant stromal features, including marked atypia, high mitotic activity (>10 per 10 HPF), stromal overgrowth, and infiltrative margins. It can metastasize (most commonly to lung and bone) and requires multidisciplinary management. Treatment involves surgical resection with wide margins (≥1 cm), mastectomy often necessary, and consideration of adjuvant radiation and systemic therapy [PHYLLSARC-2].
tier_2_handbook.malignant_phyllodes_subtypes: Malignant phyllodes tumors are a high-grade subtype of phyllodes tumors. Features include mitoses >10 per 10 HPF, severe cellular atypia, stromal overgrowth, and malignant heterologous elements (e.g., liposarcoma, chondrosarcoma, osteosarcoma) [PHYLLSARC-2 footnote d]. The guideline does not further subclassify malignant phyllodes.
tier_2_handbook.malignant_phyllodes_molecular_pathogenes: The molecular pathogenesis of malignant phyllodes tumors is not detailed in the guideline. Genetic counseling and testing for breast and sarcoma genes are recommended [PHYLLSARC-2 footnote a]. The guideline does not list specific molecular aberrations.
tier_2_handbook.malignant_phyllodes_epidemiology: Malignant phyllodes tumors are rare. The guideline does not provide incidence or mortality numbers. A multicenter retrospective study of 51 patients with metastatic malignant phyllodes reported that surgery of metastasis was independently associated with overall survival (HR 0.33; 95% CI 0.14–0.78) [PHYLLSARC-4A footnote q]. Lung, bone, and visceral metastases are common.
tier_2_handbook.malignant_phyllodes_risk_factors: No specific risk factors are identified. Genetic syndromes (e.g., Li-Fraumeni) may be relevant based on personal/family history [PHYLLSARC-2 footnote a].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Soft tissue sarcomas (STS) most commonly present as a painless, enlarging mass located in the extremities (43% of cases), trunk (10%), visceral organs (19%), retroperitoneum (15%), or head and neck (9%) [EXTSARC-1, MS-2]. The differential diagnosis includes benign lesions such as lipomas, desmoid tumors, and other malignant entities. Retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal STS often present insidiously with abdominal fullness, pain, or incidental findings on imaging [RETSARC-1]. Desmoid tumors (aggressive fibromatosis) present as firm, often painless, locally invasive masses that rarely metastasize, with a tendency for local recurrence; they may occur in the abdominal wall, intra-abdominal mesentery, extremities, or chest wall [DESM-1, MS-30]. Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) in adults most commonly arises in the extremities (26%), trunk (23%), genitourinary tract (17%), and head and neck (9%) and can present as a rapidly growing mass [RMS-1, MS-34]. Pleomorphic RMS is rare and behaves like high-grade STS. In children and adolescents, RMS is the most common STS, with embryonal and alveolar histologies. Atypical lipomatous tumor/well-differentiated liposarcoma (ALT/WDLPS) of the extremity, trunk, or abdominal wall presents as a slow-growing, deep-seated mass; retroperitoneal WDLPS may be found incidentally [ALT/WDLPS-1]. Borderline and malignant phyllodes tumors of the breast present as rapidly enlarging breast masses, often with skin changes, and require differentiation from breast carcinoma [PHYLLSARC-1, PHYLLSARC-2].

symptoms.name: Palpable mass
symptoms.detail: The most common presenting symptom is a painless, enlarging soft tissue mass. In retroperitoneal sarcomas, the mass may not be palpable until large, with symptoms of abdominal discomfort, early satiety, or back pain.
symptoms.alarm: Rapid growth over weeks to months, or new onset of pain, warrants urgent investigation.

symptoms.name: Pain
symptoms.detail: Pain may be present if the tumor compresses or invades nerves, bone, or adjacent structures. Pain is more common in high-grade lesions and in retroperitoneal sarcomas.
symptoms.frequency: Variable; up to one-third of patients
symptoms.alarm: Persistent, unexplained pain, especially at night or at rest, necessitates imaging.

symptoms.name: Functional impairment
symptoms.detail: Extremity sarcomas may cause limited range of motion, neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), or vascular compromise if located near neurovascular bundles.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; depends on tumor location and size
symptoms.alarm: Acute neurological deficit or vascular insufficiency requires emergency evaluation.

symptoms.name: Systemic symptoms
symptoms.detail: Rarely, large retroperitoneal or visceral sarcomas may cause weight loss, anorexia, or fatigue. Advanced/metastatic disease may produce cough, dyspnea (lung metastases), or abdominal pain (liver metastases).
symptoms.alarm: Unexplained weight loss, fever, night sweats should prompt staging.

symptoms.name: Breast mass (phyllodes tumor)
symptoms.detail: Phyllodes tumors present as a firm, painless breast mass that can grow rapidly. Malignant phyllodes may cause skin ulceration or chest wall invasion.
symptoms.alarm: Rapid growth, skin changes, or size >5 cm require urgent workup.

signs.name: Palpable mass on physical exam
signs.detail: Firm, often fixed to underlying structures, may be tender. Superficial masses are more easily detected; deep-seated retroperitoneal masses may require imaging for detection.
signs.frequency: Nearly all extremity/trunk STS; less frequent for retroperitoneal

signs.name: Neurologic deficits
signs.detail: Motor weakness, sensory loss, or reflex changes may be present if the tumor involves major nerves (e.g., sciatic nerve in thigh sarcomas).

signs.name: Vascular compromise
signs.detail: Edema, pallor, or diminished pulses can occur with compression or invasion of major vessels.

signs.name: Abdominal mass or organomegaly
signs.detail: Retroperitoneal sarcomas may present with a palpable abdominal mass on deep palpation. Hepatomegaly or splenomegaly may be seen with metastatic disease.

signs.name: Breast changes (phyllodes)
signs.detail: Firm, well-circumscribed breast mass; may have overlying skin dimpling or ulceration. Nipple discharge is rare.

atypical_or_missed: Painless mass dismissed as a lipoma or hematoma, especially in the extremities, leading to delayed diagnosis.
Retroperitoneal sarcomas frequently misdiagnosed as benign ovarian cysts, uterine fibroids, or abdominal wall hernias due to nonspecific symptoms.
Desmoid tumors may be mistaken for scar tissue or recurrent cancer after surgery.
Phyllodes tumors may be misclassified as fibroadenomas on core needle biopsy due to sampling error.

red_flags: Rapidly enlarging soft tissue mass over weeks to months
Mass with new onset of pain, especially at rest or at night
Neurological deficit (motor weakness, sensory loss) associated with a mass
Acute vascular compromise (pallor, pulselessness, cool extremity) with a mass
Unexplained systemic symptoms (weight loss, fever, night sweats) in the setting of a soft tissue mass
Breast mass with rapid growth, skin changes, or size >5 cm (suspicious for malignant phyllodes)
Intra-abdominal mass causing obstructive symptoms or ascites
Previous radiation therapy to the area
Personal or family history of Li-Fraumeni syndrome, neurofibromatosis, familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), or hereditary retinoblastoma
Fungating, necrotic, bleeding, or superinfected tumor (may preclude preoperative radiation/chemotherapy) [EXTSARC-3A footnote t]

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for baseline assessment, detection of mass characteristics, and identification of hereditary syndromes.
diagnostic.key_findings: Firm, fixed mass; neurological or vascular deficits; abdominal mass; skin changes in phyllodes tumors.

diagnostic.rationale: Provides details about tumor size, depth, relationship to neurovascular structures and adjacent organs.
diagnostic.key_findings: MRI with and without contrast (preferred for extremity/body wall, head and neck, and cardiac) or CT with contrast (preferred for retroperitoneum) [SARC-A, 1 of 5].

diagnostic.test: Core needle biopsy (preferred) or incisional biopsy
diagnostic.rationale: Establishes malignancy, histologic subtype, and grade. Image-guided core needle biopsy is preferred for deep-seated tumors.
diagnostic.key_findings: Should be performed after adequate imaging; biopsy tract placed along future resection axis with minimal dissection and careful hemostasis [EXTSARC-1, SARC-D 1 of 4]. Biopsy should establish grade and histologic subtype using standardized nomenclature (WHO Classification) [SARC-B].

diagnostic.test: Ancillary diagnostic techniques (IHC, cytogenetics, molecular testing)
diagnostic.rationale: Necessary for definitive diagnosis when morphology is equivocal; many STS subtypes harbor characteristic genetic aberrations.
diagnostic.key_findings: IHC, FISH, PCR, NGS used to identify translocations (e.g., EWSR1::FLI1 in Ewing sarcoma, SS18::SSX in synovial sarcoma), amplifications (MDM2 in WDLPS/ddLPS), or mutations (CTNNB1 in desmoid tumors) [SARC-C 1-4].

diagnostic.rationale: Useful in staging, prognostication, grading, and determining response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
diagnostic.key_findings: FDG-PET/CT is more common; FDG-PET/MRI may be considered. May differentiate benign from malignant lesions (e.g., neurofibroma vs MPNST in NF1) [SARC-A, 1 of 5].

diagnostic.rationale: May be warranted in selected circumstances (e.g., to evaluate bone involvement or for long-term surveillance to minimize radiation).
diagnostic.key_findings: Can show calcifications, periosteal reaction, or bone destruction.

diagnostic.rationale: Useful for surgical planning when major vascular involvement is suspected.
diagnostic.key_findings: Identifies vessel displacement, encasement, or invasion.

staging.test: Chest imaging (CT chest without contrast preferred, or plain radiographs)
staging.rationale: Essential to evaluate for pulmonary metastases, which is the most common site of distant spread for most STS subtypes.
staging.indication: All STS except well-differentiated liposarcoma (WDLPS) [SARC-A, 1-2 of 5].

staging.rationale: Necessary for retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal STS; also indicated for specific histologies with propensity for abdominal/pelvic metastases (e.g., myxoid/round cell liposarcoma, LMS, epithelioid sarcoma).
staging.indication: All retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas; for extremity/trunk sarcomas with certain histologies (angiosarcoma, LMS, myxoid liposarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma) [SARC-A, 2-3 of 5].

staging.test: MRI brain with and without contrast (or CT brain if MRI contraindicated)
staging.rationale: To evaluate for CNS metastases in histologies with high propensity for brain spread.
staging.indication: Alveolar soft part sarcoma (ASPS), angiosarcoma, cardiac sarcoma (left-sided), and malignant phyllodes (consider symptom-driven) [SARC-A, 1-2 of 5].

staging.rationale: Myxoid/round cell liposarcoma has a higher risk of spine metastases.

staging.test: Whole body MRI or FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI)
staging.rationale: To identify soft tissue metastases or bone metastases in histologies with unusual metastatic patterns.
staging.indication: Myxoid/round cell liposarcoma (WB MRI preferred); ASPS, angiosarcoma, clear cell sarcoma, LMS, RMS (consider FDG-PET or bone scan for bone metastases) [SARC-A, 2-3 of 5].

staging.test: Regional lymph node assessment (CT, FDG-PET, or sentinel node biopsy)
staging.rationale: Certain histologies have a propensity for nodal metastases (e.g., clear cell sarcoma, angiosarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma, alveolar RMS). Sentinel node staging can be considered in select cases.
staging.indication: Histologies with possible occult lymph node metastasis; multidisciplinary discussion recommended [SARC-D 1 of 4, SARC-A 2-3 of 5].

staging.rationale: To evaluate for bone metastases in histologies with skeletal tropism.
staging.indication: Consider for angiosarcoma, ASPS, clear cell sarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma [SARC-A, 2 of 5].

staging.test: Breast MRI with and without contrast
staging.rationale: Required for staging and surgical planning in phyllodes tumors; also for surveillance.
staging.indication: All phyllodes tumors (borderline and malignant) [PHYLLSARC-1, PHYLLSARC-2].

biomarkers.test: NTRK gene fusion testing (NGS or FISH)
biomarkers.rationale: Identifies patients with NTRK fusion-positive sarcomas who may benefit from TRK inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib).
biomarkers.when: In advanced/metastatic disease regardless of subtype; considered for first-line therapy in NTRK fusion-positive tumors.

biomarkers.rationale: Pembrolizumab is approved for TMB-H solid tumors that have progressed and have no satisfactory alternative.
biomarkers.mandatory: FDA-approved test required for pembrolizumab eligibility per indication.

biomarkers.rationale: Pembrolizumab is approved for MSI-H or dMMR tumors.
biomarkers.mandatory: Required for pembrolizumab eligibility per indication.

biomarkers.test: ALK translocation testing (by IHC, FISH, or NGS)
biomarkers.rationale: Identifies inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor (IMT) with ALK translocation, which is responsive to ALK inhibitors (alectinib, brigatinib, ceritinib, crizotinib, lorlatinib).
biomarkers.when: In IMT histology, especially if advanced/unresectable.

biomarkers.test: MDM2 and CDK4 amplification (by FISH or NGS)
biomarkers.rationale: Confirms diagnosis of well-differentiated/dedifferentiated liposarcoma and identifies potential CDK4 inhibitor candidates (palbociclib, abemaciclib).
biomarkers.when: In lipomatous tumors with equivocal histology.
biomarkers.mandatory: Useful for diagnosis; not mandatory for treatment decisions.

biomarkers.rationale: Sporadic desmoid tumors often have CTNNB1 mutations; APC mutations indicate FAP/Gardner syndrome.
biomarkers.when: In desmoid tumors to confirm diagnosis and assess hereditary risk.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended for diagnostic confirmation and genetic counseling.

biomarkers.rationale: Selpercatinib is active in RET fusion-positive solid tumors, including sarcoma.
Afamitresgene autoleucel (T-cell therapy) is approved for synovial sarcoma with specific HLA types and MAGE-A4 expression.
biomarkers.when: In advanced synovial sarcoma after prior therapy.

do_not_miss: Core needle biopsy with adequate tissue for histology and grade
Chest CT for staging of all STS (except WDLPS of retroperitoneum)
MRI of primary site for extremity/body wall/head and neck sarcomas
Multidisciplinary tumor board review prior to treatment initiation
Assessment for cancer predisposition syndromes (Li-Fraumeni, NF1, FAP, Lynch, hereditary retinoblastoma) based on history [EXTSARC-1A, SARC-H]
Distress assessment using NCCN Distress Thermometer [EXTSARC-1]
FDG-PET/CT for staging of RMS due to nodal and unusual metastatic sites [RMS-1]
MRI brain for ASPS and angiosarcoma due to CNS metastasis risk
Total spine MRI for myxoid/round cell liposarcoma
avoid: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) as initial diagnostic tool due to insufficient tissue for accurate subclassification [MS-4]
Biopsy before adequate imaging (risk of missing critical planning details)
Unplanned excision without prior staging (risk of contaminated margins and increased LR)
Routine sentinel node biopsy in histologies without known nodal involvement (insufficient evidence) [SARC-D 1 of 4]
Adjuvant RT for routine management of retroperitoneal sarcoma (discouraged) [SARC-E 3 of 6]
Systemic therapy for low-grade retroperitoneal liposarcoma without dedifferentiation [RETSARC-2]

staging.system: American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017) – TNM classification with histologic grade (FNCLCC). Separate staging systems exist for: (1) head and neck, (2) trunk and extremities, (3) abdomen and thoracic visceral organs, (4) retroperitoneum. Grade is determined by the FNCLCC system (tumor differentiation, mitotic count, necrosis) [ST-4 through ST-7].

t_stages.description: No evidence of primary tumor (for trunk/extremities and retroperitoneum). Not defined for head/neck.
Trunk/extremities, retroperitoneum: Tumor ≤5 cm in greatest dimension. Head/neck: Tumor ≤2 cm.
Trunk/extremities, retroperitoneum: Tumor >5 cm and ≤10 cm. Head/neck: Tumor >2 cm to ≤4 cm.
Trunk/extremities, retroperitoneum: Tumor >10 cm and ≤15 cm. Head/neck: Tumor >4 cm (no further size subdivision).

Trunk/extremities, retroperitoneum: Tumor >15 cm. Head/neck: Tumor with invasion of adjoining structures – T4a: orbital, skull base/dural, central compartment viscera, facial skeleton, pterygoid muscles; T4b: brain parenchyma, carotid artery encasement, prevertebral muscle invasion, CNS perineural spread.
Abdomen/thoracic visceral organs: Invades serosa or visceral peritoneum.
Abdomen/thoracic visceral organs: Extension beyond serosa (mesentery).

n_stages.description: No regional lymph node metastasis or unknown lymph node status.
Regional lymph node metastasis (for all sites).

stage_groups.criteria: T1, N0, M0, G1 or GX (for trunk/extremities: T1 N0 M0 G1/GX; for head/neck: T1 N0 M0 G1/GX; for retroperitoneum: T1 N0 M0 G1/GX). For abdomen/thoracic visceral organs, no stage grouping is recommended.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Low-grade, small tumor with good prognosis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated in source; survival data not provided in the staging tables.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – surgical resection alone or with selective RT.

stage_groups.criteria: T2, T3, or T4, N0, M0, G1/GX (trunk/extremities and retroperitoneum). For head/neck: T2-4 N0 M0 G1/GX.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Low-grade, larger tumor but still low grade, favorable biology.

stage_groups.criteria: Trunk/extremities and retroperitoneum: T1 N0 M0 G2 or G3. For head/neck: T1 N0 M0 G2 or G3. (Note: For abdomen/thoracic visceral organs, no stage grouping.)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-grade, small tumor; moderate risk of metastasis.

stage_groups.criteria: Trunk/extremities and retroperitoneum: T2 N0 M0 G2/G3.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-grade, moderate-sized tumor; increased risk of recurrence and metastasis.

stage_groups.criteria: Trunk/extremities and retroperitoneum: T3 or T4 N0 M0 G2/G3. For head/neck: T2-4 N0 M0 G2/G3 (no further subdivision).
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-grade, large or locally invasive tumor; high risk of recurrence and metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – multimodality therapy; neoadjuvant approaches preferred.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, M0, any grade (any site) OR any T, any N, M1, any grade.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Regional lymph node involvement or distant metastases. Prognosis is poor.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated; median survival for metastatic disease is approximately 12-18 months based on clinical data cited in the discussion (MS-19).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative – systemic therapy, local therapies for oligometastases, best supportive care.

staging_pearls: AJCC 8th edition uses a single classification for T based on size (except head/neck where invasion defines T4). Grade (FNCLCC) is the dominant prognostic factor, dividing stage groups primarily by grade.
For head and neck, there is no recommended stage grouping (data collection ongoing) [ST-4].
For abdomen and thoracic visceral organs, no prognostic stage grouping is recommended [ST-6].

The FNCLCC grade is calculated from scores for differentiation (1-3), mitotic count (1-3: 0-9/10 HPF = 1, 10-19 = 2, ≥20 = 3), and necrosis (0: no necrosis, 1: <50%, 2: ≥50%). Total score: G1 = 2-3, G2 = 4-5, G3 = 6-8 [ST-4].
Lymph node involvement (N1) automatically assigns stage IV regardless of T or G.
For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas, T4 is defined by multifocality (T4a: 2 sites, T4b: 3-5 sites, T4c: >5 sites) [ST-6].
The guidelines reference AJCC 8th edition but note that new staging systems may be updated (e.g., AJCC 9th edition anticipated). Always verify current edition.

management_principles.overview: The management of soft tissue sarcoma (STS) is founded on a multidisciplinary, multimodality treatment philosophy. Prior to the initiation of therapy, all patients should be evaluated and treated by a multidisciplinary team with expertise and experience in sarcoma management [EXTSARC-1]. This team should include specialists in surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, radiology, and rehabilitation. The anatomic site of the primary tumor (extremity/body wall, head/neck, retroperitoneum/intra-abdominal) and histologic subtype are critical determinants of treatment strategy [MS-2]. The treatment philosophy emphasizes individualized care based on stage, grade, resectability, functional outcomes, and patient preferences. Limb-sparing surgery with negative margins is the primary goal for extremity tumors, with amputation reserved for cases where gross total resection would render the limb nonfunctional or patient preference [SARC-D 1 of 4]. For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas, complete surgical resection with oncologically appropriate margins is the primary curative approach, but given proximity to critical structures, close margins may be necessary [RETSARC-2]. Neoadjuvant and adjuvant radiation therapy and systemic therapy are integrated to improve local control and reduce recurrence risk, particularly for high-grade or large tumors. For desmoid tumors, initial observation is appropriate given the potential for spontaneous regression, while active therapy is reserved for progression causing morbidity [DESM-2, DESM-3]. The treatment philosophy for rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) requires risk-stratified multimodality therapy, and referral to centers with pediatric oncology expertise is strongly recommended [RMS-1]. For borderline/malignant phyllodes tumors, surgical excision with ≥1 cm margins is the cornerstone, with adjuvant radiation considered for high-risk features [PHYLLSARC-1, PHYLLSARC-2]. The overall approach integrates evidence-based recommendations with the recognition that STS is rare and heterogeneous, requiring expert multidisciplinary input.

by_intent.patient_group: Stage I, II, III, and select Stage IV (any T, N1, M0) resectable with acceptable functional outcomes
by_intent.approach: Surgery to obtain oncologically appropriate margins is the primary treatment. For stage I (low grade), surgery alone is often sufficient; for stage II/III (high grade), surgery is combined with neoadjuvant or adjuvant radiation therapy (category 1 for preoperative RT) with or without systemic therapy. For select histologies (e.g., UPS-related sarcomas), neoadjuvant pembrolizumab with RT may be considered [EXTSARC-3, SARC-G 1 of 14]. For retroperitoneal sarcoma, neoadjuvant RT may be considered in selected high-risk cases [RETSARC-2]. For localized malignant phyllodes, surgical resection with ≥1 cm margins followed by consideration of adjuvant RT is the approach [PHYLLSARC-2].

by_intent.patient_group: Stage II/III resectable but with unacceptable functional outcomes or unresectable primary disease
by_intent.approach: Neoadjuvant RT or systemic therapy is used to attempt downstaging to allow for function-preserving surgery. If response allows, resection is performed. If not, options include amputation, definitive RT, isolated limb perfusion/infusion (in experienced centers), or palliative treatments [EXTSARC-4].

by_intent.patient_group: Stage IV (synchronous or recurrent) with disseminated metastases or oligometastatic disease not amenable to local therapy
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy is the mainstay. For oligometastatic disease with limited tumor bulk, local therapies such as metastasectomy, SBRT, ablation, or embolization can be considered. For disseminated disease, palliative systemic therapy, RT, symptom-directed surgery, or best supportive care are options. Observation is appropriate for asymptomatic patients with indolent tumor biology [EXTSARC-5, EXTSARC-6, RETSARC-5].

by_intent.patient_group: Desmoid tumors in anatomic locations where progression would not be morbid, or stable/regressing tumors
by_intent.approach: Initial observation with imaging every 3 months is recommended. Spontaneous regression occurs in up to 20% of patients. Therapy (systemic therapy, surgery, ablation, RT) is initiated only for documented progression with potential morbidity or significant symptoms [DESM-2, DESM-3, DESM-4].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: All patients should be evaluated and treated by a multidisciplinary team with expertise and experience in sarcoma management [EXTSARC-1]. The multidisciplinary surgical team should include expertise in the management of sarcomas at specific anatomic sites as well as reconstruction [SARC-D 1 of 4]. For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas, a multidisciplinary sarcoma panel should review the patient if possible [RETSARC-1]. For RMS, multidisciplinary evaluation involving pediatric, medical, surgical, and radiation oncologists is strongly encouraged, and referral to institutions with expertise in treating RMS is strongly recommended [RMS-1]. For desmoid tumors, all patients should be managed by a multidisciplinary team with expertise and experience in desmoid tumors [DESM-1]. For phyllodes tumors, a multidisciplinary team with expertise and experience in phyllodes tumor is required [PHYLLSARC-1, PHYLLSARC-2].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status is considered in treatment decisions, particularly for advanced/metastatic disease where it influences the choice of systemic therapy, intensity of local therapies, and suitability for surgery. The guideline references performance status in selection of patients for metastasectomy, SBRT, and palliative RT [EXTSARC-5, EXTSARC-6, RETSARC-5]. Pazopanib is a first-line option for patients aged ≥60 years or those ineligible for IV systemic therapy [SARC-G 1 of 14]. For unresectable disease, observation is an option for asymptomatic patients with indolent tumor biology [EXTSARC-4, RETSARC-4]. The panel recommends preoperative assessment of medical comorbidities, physical endurance/frailty, and nutritional status prior to surgery [SARC-D 3 of 4].

management_pathways.title: Extremity/Body Wall/Head and Neck Soft Tissue Sarcoma – Stage I (Low Grade)
branching_basis: Tumor stage (IA T1≤5 cm, IB T2-4)

nodes.criteria: Stage IA (T1, N0, M0, low grade) or Stage IB (T2-4, N0, M0, low grade) – Primary disease

options.label: Surgery to obtain oncologically appropriate margins
options.recommendation: Surgical wide resection with intent to obtain negative margins is definitive. If margins ≤1 cm without intact fascial plane, re-resection may be necessary [EXTSARC-2, SARC-D 2 of 4]. In rare situations where surgery is not an option, consider observation [EXTSARC-2 footnote l].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If final surgical margins ≤1 cm and without intact fascial plane: re-resection or consider adjuvant RT (category 2B for stage IA, category 1 for stage IB). Observation is an option for stage IA with adequate margins. For patients with ALT/WDLPS and focally positive margins, observation is recommended if re-resection in the event of recurrence would not be unduly morbid [SARC-D 2 of 4].

management_pathways.title: Extremity/Body Wall/Head and Neck – Stage II/III or Select Stage IV Resectable with Acceptable Functional Outcomes
branching_basis: Stage (II, III, select IV [any T, N1, M0])

nodes.criteria: Stage II (T1, N0, M0, G2-3) or Stage III (T2-4, N0, M0, G2-3) or select Stage IV (any T, N1, M0) – resectable with acceptable functional outcomes

options.label: Neoadjuvant RT (category 1) + Surgery + Adjuvant systemic therapy (consider)
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant RT is preferred. For select histologies (UPS, MFS, ddLPS, pleomorphic LPS), neoadjuvant pembrolizumab with RT followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab may be considered [SARC-G 1 of 14].

options.label: Surgery + RT (category 1) ± Adjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Postoperative RT requires higher doses and larger fields. For positive margins after preoperative RT, consider observation or RT boost in select situations [SARC-E 2 of 6].

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy + RT + Surgery + Adjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy may be beneficial in chemotherapy-sensitive tumor types with high probability of mortality (e.g., FNCLCC Grade 3, large tumors >10 cm) [EXTSARC-3 footnote v].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider adjuvant systemic therapy for high-risk features (high grade, large size, positive margins). For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas, adjuvant RT is not routinely recommended except for highly selected patients where local recurrence would cause undue morbidity [RETSARC-3]. Adjuvant RT should not be administered routinely for retroperitoneal sarcoma [SARC-E 3 of 6].

management_pathways.title: Extremity/Body Wall/Head and Neck – Stage II/III Resectable with Unacceptable Functional Outcomes or Unresectable Primary Disease

nodes.criteria: Resectable but with unacceptable functional outcomes

options.label: Neoadjuvant RT or systemic therapy + RT to downstage
options.recommendation: If downstaging successful, consider surgery + RT. If not downstaging, options include amputation/radical resection (if acceptable) or definitive RT [EXTSARC-4].

options.label: Amputation/radical resection
options.recommendation: Consider if gross total resection can be achieved with functional outcome acceptable to patient. Prior to amputation, evaluation by a surgeon with sarcoma expertise is recommended [SARC-D 1 of 4].

options.label: Isolated limb perfusion/infusion
options.recommendation: Should only be performed at institutions with experience in this technique [EXTSARC-4 footnote aa].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If surgery is performed after neoadjuvant therapy, consider adjuvant systemic therapy for high-risk features. If not previously irradiated, consider definitive RT.

nodes.criteria: Unresectable primary disease

options.label: Definitive RT (if not previously irradiated)
options.recommendation: Doses of ≥63 Gy, up to 70-80 Gy based on normal tissue tolerance. Long-term local control from radiation alone may be inferior to multimodality therapy but is reasonable for non-surgical candidates [SARC-E 2 of 6].

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Anthracycline-based regimens preferred as first-line. Consider pazopanib for patients ineligible for IV therapy [SARC-G 1 of 14].

options.label: Observation, if asymptomatic
options.recommendation: For asymptomatic patients with indolent tumor biology, observation is appropriate [EXTSARC-4].

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: For symptomatic patients not candidates for other therapies [EXTSARC-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for unresectable disease.

management_pathways.title: Extremity/Body Wall/Head and Neck – Synchronous Stage IV Disease
branching_basis: Extent of metastatic disease (oligometastatic vs. disseminated)

nodes.criteria: Oligometastases with limited tumor bulk amenable to local therapy

options.label: Systemic therapy for all patients
options.recommendation: Anthracycline-based regimens or histology-specific therapy. Primary tumor managed per EXTSARC-3/EXTSARC-4 [EXTSARC-5].

options.label: Metastasectomy ± RT
options.recommendation: Metastasectomy is the historical standard for oligometastatic disease (primarily lung). Choice depends on performance status, lesion accessibility, and anticipated morbidity [EXTSARC-5 footnote bb].

options.label: RT/SBRT
options.recommendation: Various SBRT dosing regimens reported effective for sarcoma metastases. Dose/fractionation determined by experienced radiation oncologist based on normal tissue constraints [EXTSARC-5 footnote cc].

options.label: Ablation or embolization procedures
options.recommendation: For non-lung metastases, embolization may be considered. Ablation techniques include thermal (cryoablation, RFA, microwave) and non-thermal (IRE) [SARC-F 2 of 3].

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: For asymptomatic, indolent disease [EXTSARC-5].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow-up imaging individualized to clinical situation.

nodes.criteria: Disseminated metastases

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: First-line anthracycline-based, or histology-specific therapy per SARC-G [EXTSARC-5].

options.label: RT/SBRT for palliation
options.recommendation: Palliative RT can improve symptoms from unresectable or metastatic disease [SARC-E 4 of 6].

options.label: Surgery for palliation
options.recommendation: Consider for symptom control.

options.label: Observation, if asymptomatic
options.recommendation: For minimal burden (e.g., sub-centimeter pulmonary nodules).

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Includes palliative care referral [NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care].

options.label: Ablation or embolization (non-lung metastases)
options.recommendation: For symptomatic lesions in liver, bone, or soft tissue [SARC-F].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow-up individualized.

management_pathways.title: Extremity/Body Wall/Head and Neck – Recurrent Disease
branching_basis: Type of recurrence (local vs. metastatic: single organ limited vs. disseminated vs isolated nodal)

nodes.criteria: Local recurrence

options.label: Follow Workup, then appropriate Primary Treatment pathway (EXTSARC-2, EXTSARC-3, or EXTSARC-4)
options.recommendation: If LR can be excised, decision on re-irradiation case-by-case. Brachytherapy, IMRT, proton beam therapy may reduce morbidity of re-irradiation [EXTSARC-6 footnote ff].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Based upon tumor biology and disease-free interval, consideration for additional chemotherapy and/or radiation made on case-by-case basis by sarcoma multidisciplinary team [EXTSARC-6 footnote gg]. If not previously irradiated, consider preoperative or postoperative RT.

nodes.criteria: Metastatic – Single organ and limited tumor bulk amenable to local therapy

options.label: Metastasectomy ± neoadjuvant or adjuvant systemic therapy ± RT
options.recommendation: Metastasectomy is historical standard for oligometastatic disease (primarily lung). Choice depends on performance status, lesion accessibility, and anticipated morbidity [EXTSARC-6 footnote bb].

options.label: SBRT ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Various SBRT dosing regimens effective for sarcoma metastases [EXTSARC-6 footnote cc].

options.label: Ablation or embolization (non-lung metastases)
options.recommendation: Consider for liver, lung, musculoskeletal metastases [SARC-F].

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: For asymptomatic, indolent disease.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy for unfavorable tumor biology (short disease-free interval, prior metastases) before local therapy [RETSARC-5 footnote x].

nodes.criteria: Metastatic – Disseminated metastases

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Subsequent-line options per SARC-G (e.g., pazopanib, trabectedin, eribulin, gemcitabine-based, immunotherapy) [SARC-G 1 of 14].

options.label: RT/SBRT for palliation
options.recommendation: Palliative RT can improve symptoms. Dose/fractionation based on normal tissue constraints, performance status, prognosis [SARC-E 4 of 6].

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Consider for symptom control.

options.label: Observation, if asymptomatic; Best supportive care
options.recommendation: For asymptomatic patients, watchful waiting is reasonable [EXTSARC-6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative intent.

nodes.criteria: Isolated regional disease or nodes

options.label: Resection of involved lymph nodes/regional nodal dissection ± RT ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Radical lymphadenectomy may provide long-term survival benefit for isolated nodal involvement [EXTSARC-6, MS-19].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT and systemic therapy considered on case-by-case basis.

management_pathways.title: Retroperitoneal/Intra-Abdominal Soft Tissue Sarcoma – Resectable Primary Disease

nodes.criteria: Resectable primary disease (sarcoma)

options.label: Surgery to obtain oncologically appropriate margins
options.recommendation: Complete resection with negative margins is primary curative approach. Surgery can often result in physical disability [RETSARC-2].

options.label: Neoadjuvant therapy (in selected cases) – RT (consider for tumors at high risk for local recurrence) ± IORT (only after neoadjuvant RT) OR Systemic therapy (if high risk for metastatic disease or downstaging needed)
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is not recommended for low-grade tumors (e.g., WDLPS with no dedifferentiation). Neoadjuvant RT can be considered for selected patients at high risk for local recurrence [RETSARC-2, SARC-E 3 of 6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After resection, adjuvant RT should not be administered routinely except for highly selected patients and unless local recurrence would cause undue morbidity. Consider adjuvant systemic therapy if high risk for metastatic disease (not recommended for low-grade tumors) [RETSARC-3].

management_pathways.title: Retroperitoneal/Intra-Abdominal – Unresectable Primary or Recurrent Disease

nodes.criteria: Unresectable primary or recurrent disease (biopsy needed for clinical decision if not previously done)

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Anthracycline-based regimens for general STS; histology-specific options per SARC-G.

options.label: RT (preoperative or definitive)
options.recommendation: If neoadjuvant RT anticipated, IMRT/IGRT/proton therapy recommended to improve therapeutic ratio. Consider IORT boost for positive margins [SARC-E 3 of 6].

options.label: Palliative surgery
options.recommendation: For symptom control.

options.label: Observation, if asymptomatic and indolent tumor biology; Best supportive care
options.recommendation: If asymptomatic and indolent, observation is appropriate [RETSARC-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If tumor becomes resectable after primary treatment, proceed with RETSARC-2 pathway.

nodes.criteria: Single organ and limited tumor bulk amenable to local therapy

options.label: Local therapies (metastasectomy, SBRT, ablation, embolization) ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Metastasectomy should be considered if primary tumor can be controlled. For unfavorable tumor biology (e.g., short disease-free interval), consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy before local therapy [RETSARC-5].

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: For asymptomatic, indolent disease.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow-up should be individualized.

nodes.criteria: Disseminated metastases

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: First-line anthracycline-based; consider pazopanib, trabectedin, eribulin, immunotherapy per SARC-G.

options.label: RT/SBRT for palliation
options.recommendation: Palliative RT can improve symptoms [SARC-E 4 of 6].

options.label: Surgery for palliation
options.recommendation: Consider for symptom control.

options.label: Observation, if asymptomatic; Best supportive care
options.recommendation: For asymptomatic patients, watchful waiting is reasonable.

options.label: Ablation or embolization (non-lung metastases)
options.recommendation: For liver-dominant disease, catheter-directed therapies (TAE, TACE, TARE) can be considered [SARC-F 1 of 3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative intent.

management_pathways.title: Desmoid Tumors (Aggressive Fibromatosis) – Anatomic Location Where Progression Would Not Be Morbid

nodes.criteria: Anatomic location where progression would not be morbid

options.label: Observation with imaging and symptom management
options.recommendation: Initial imaging every 3 months recommended. Spontaneous regression reported in up to 20% of patients, supporting initial observation [DESM-2, footnote e].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If stable/regression, continue observation. If progression, consider ongoing observation if minimally symptomatic and location not critical, or proceed to active therapy (DESM-4) [DESM-2].

management_pathways.title: Desmoid Tumors – Anatomic Location Where Progression Would Be Morbid, or Documented Progression with Morbidity/Symptoms
branching_basis: Anatomic location (intra-abdominal/retroperitoneal vs. all other sites)

nodes.criteria: Intra-abdominal/retroperitoneal

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Options include nirogacestat (category 1), sorafenib (category 1), methotrexate/vinorelbine, methotrexate/vinblastine, imatinib, liposomal doxorubicin, doxorubicin, dacarbazine/doxorubicin, pazopanib [DESM-4, SARC-G 2 of 14].

options.label: Surgery (less preferred)
options.recommendation: Surgery is not considered first-line except in certain situations if agreed upon by multidisciplinary tumor board. Can often result in physical disability [DESM-4, footnote h, i].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For R0/R1/R2, options include definitive RT, systemic therapy, ablation/embolization, observation, or radical surgery if other modalities fail. Adjuvant RT not routinely recommended [DESM-4].

nodes.criteria: All other sites (abdominal wall, pelvis, trunk/extremity, head/neck/intrathoracic)

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Same options as intra-abdominal/retroperitoneal [SARC-G 2 of 14].

options.label: Ablation/embolization
options.recommendation: Cryoablation is the most commonly utilized ablation modality. DEB chemoembolization can be used for extra-abdominal desmoids if cryoablation is not technically possible [SARC-F 2 of 3].

options.label: Definitive RT
options.recommendation: Recommended dose: 56 Gy in 28 fractions. Treat similarly to high-grade STS given infiltrative nature [SARC-E 2 of 6].

options.label: Surgery (less preferred)
options.recommendation: Not first-line except in certain situations. If performed, wide excision with ≥1 cm margin. Adjuvant RT is not routinely recommended following R0/R1 resection [DESM-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For R0: observation. For R1: observation or re-resection. For R2: definitive RT, systemic therapy, ablation/embolization, observation, or radical surgery if other modalities fail. Consider ± RT for head/neck/intrathoracic lesions where recurrence would be technically challenging [DESM-4].

nodes.criteria: Pleomorphic RMS

options.label: Recommend treating like high-grade soft tissue sarcoma
options.recommendation: See SARC-G for systemic therapy options appropriate for general STS (e.g., anthracycline-based regimens). Pleomorphic RMS is not to be confused with anaplastic variant in children [RMS-1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Multimodality treatment with surgery, RT, and chemotherapy as per high-grade STS guidelines.

management_pathways.title: Rhabdomyosarcoma – Non-Pleomorphic RMS (Alveolar, Embryonal, Spindle Cell/Sclerosing)

nodes.criteria: Non-pleomorphic RMS (includes alveolar, embryonal, spindle cell/sclerosing with VGLL2-related fusions, intraosseous, or MYOD1 mutation)

options.label: Referral to institutions with expertise in treating RMS is strongly recommended. Multidisciplinary evaluation involving pediatric, medical, surgical, and radiation oncologists is strongly encouraged
options.recommendation: Multimodality treatment planning and risk stratification is required. Systemic therapy options may be different than those used with other STS histologies [RMS-1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy per pediatric protocols: preferred regimens include VAC (vincristine/dactinomycin/cyclophosphamide), VAI-Europe (vincristine/dactinomycin/ifosfamide). Other recommended: VDC-IE, cyclophosphamide/doxorubicin/vincristine, doxorubicin/ifosfamide/vincristine, etc. For intermediate-risk, consider maintenance chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide/vinorelbine for 6 months [SARC-G 2 of 14].

management_pathways.title: Atypical Lipomatous Tumor / Well-Differentiated Liposarcoma – Extremity, Abdominal Wall, Trunk

nodes.criteria: ALT/WDLPS of extremity, abdominal wall, trunk – no evidence of dedifferentiation

options.label: Surgical resection with oncologically appropriate margins
options.recommendation: ALT of extremity/trunk is generally more indolent; marginal resection technique should be tailored to minimize surgical morbidity. En bloc resection with negative margins is generally sufficient for long-term local control [ALT/WDLPS-1, footnote a].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For focally positive margins, observation is recommended if re-resection would not result in undue morbidity. RT is reserved for selected patients with recurrent or deeply infiltrative primary lesions with risk of local recurrence, depending on tumor location and patient's age [ALT/WDLPS-1, footnote b].

nodes.criteria: Evidence of dedifferentiation

options.label: Treat as other soft tissue sarcomas per EXTSARC-1
options.recommendation: Dedifferentiated liposarcoma carries higher metastatic potential and requires multidisciplinary management per extremity/body wall/head/neck sarcoma algorithms [ALT/WDLPS-1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy considerations per high-grade STS guidelines. For advanced disease, preferred regimens include anthracycline-based and gemcitabine-based regimens. Eribulin, dacarbazine, trabectedin are other recommended options. Palbociclib or abemaciclib may be useful in certain circumstances [SARC-G 6 of 14].

nodes.criteria: WDLPS of retroperitoneum or paratesticular region with or without dedifferentiation

options.label: Treat as other soft tissue sarcomas per RETSARC-1
options.recommendation: Retroperitoneal WDLPS is managed according to retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma algorithms. If dedifferentiated, treat as high-grade retroperitoneal sarcoma. Systemic therapy is not recommended for low-grade tumors (WDLPS with no dedifferentiation) [ALT/WDLPS-1, RETSARC-2 footnote n].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT not routinely recommended. Surveillance: H&P and imaging every 6-12 months for 2 yrs, then annually. Routine chest imaging for metastatic disease is not necessary for WDLPS [ALT/WDLPS-1, footnote d].

management_pathways.title: Borderline Phyllodes Tumor of the Breast

nodes.criteria: Borderline phyllodes tumor – localized resectable

options.label: Surgery to obtain oncologically appropriate margins, initial intent for 1 cm margin
options.recommendation: Wide excision means excision with intention of obtaining surgical margins ≥1 cm. Narrow margins associated with increased local recurrence risk, but not absolute indication for mastectomy. Mastectomy may be needed to obtain these margins [PHYLLSARC-1, footnote b].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If negative margins after re-excision (or initial excision), consider adjuvant RT for increased risk of local recurrence (mitoses >30/10 hpf, size >10 cm, malignant heterologous elements, multiple recurrences, narrow margins due to chest wall). Radiation therapy is not a substitute for negative margins [PHYLLSARC-1, footnote d]. For positive margins, re-excision to obtain negative margins [PHYLLSARC-1].

management_pathways.title: Malignant Phyllodes Tumor of the Breast
branching_basis: Resectability (localized resectable vs. unresectable vs. synchronous Stage IV)

nodes.criteria: Localized resectable disease

options.label: Surgical resection (preferred) with 1 cm margin, expedite initial excision without delay for staging scans or immediate breast-specific reconstruction due to rapid growth. Mastectomy without axillary staging OR breast conservation surgery with oncologically appropriate margins without axillary staging
options.recommendation: Mastectomy: due to high local recurrence rates with narrow/positive margins, attempts at nipple/skin sparing approach close to tumor is discouraged. Goal is widely negative margins. Breast conservation: adjuvant RT is recommended regardless of margin status if patient has undergone breast conservation surgery [PHYLLSARC-2, PHYLLSARC-2A footnotes b, i, k].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Negative margins: adjuvant RT following breast conservation surgery; consider adjuvant RT after mastectomy for high-risk features (mitoses >30/10 hpf, size >10 cm, malignant heterologous elements). Positive margins: additional surgical resection to obtain negative margins. Consider adjuvant systemic therapy if high risk for occult metastatic disease [PHYLLSARC-2, PHYLLSARC-2A footnotes d, h, l].

nodes.criteria: Unresectable primary disease

options.label: Definitive RT
options.recommendation: Consider preoperative RT in marginally unresectable disease or when anticipated narrow margins despite radical resection. Effort should be made to expedite initiation of preoperative RT [PHYLLSARC-2, PHYLLSARC-2A footnote f].

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Per SARC-G for general STS.

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Consider for patients not candidates for other therapies.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

nodes.criteria: Synchronous Stage IV disease (oligometastatic or disseminated)

options.label: Primary tumor management as per PHYLLSARC-2; for metastases: consider systemic therapy for all patients, metastasectomy ± RT, RT/SBRT, ablation, embolization (non-lung metastases)
options.recommendation: For oligometastases, local control modalities depend on performance status, lesion accessibility. For disseminated metastases, palliative options include systemic therapy, RT/SBRT, surgery, ablation, embolization, best supportive care [PHYLLSARC-4, PHYLLSARC-4A].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow-up includes H&P and imaging (CT C/A/P and breast/chest MRI) every 3-6 mo for 3 yrs, then every 6 mo to 5 yrs, then annually. Consider symptom-driven brain MRI for 5 yrs for malignant phyllodes [PHYLLSARC-3].

items.text: Prior to initiation of therapy, all patients should be evaluated and treated by a multidisciplinary team with expertise and experience in sarcoma [EXTSARC-1, RETSARC-1, DESM-1].
History and physical (H&P) examination [EXTSARC-1, RETSARC-1, DESM-1, RMS-1, PHYLLSARC-1, PHYLLSARC-2].

Adequate imaging of the primary tumor: MRI (preferred for extremity/body wall, head and neck, cardiac) and/or CT (preferred for retroperitoneum) with and without contrast. CT angiogram and plain radiographs may be useful in certain circumstances [EXTSARC-1 footnote b, SARC-A 1 of 5, SARC-A 3 of 5].

Image-guided core needle biopsy (preferred) or incisional biopsy to establish grade and histologic subtype. Biopsy should be performed after adequate imaging, placed along future resection axis with minimal dissection and careful attention to hemostasis [EXTSARC-1, SARC-D 1 of 4].
Ancillary diagnostic methodologies (IHC, cytogenetics, molecular genetic testing) as appropriate [EXTSARC-1, SARC-C].

Imaging of potential sites of metastatic disease: chest imaging (CT chest without contrast preferred or plain radiographs). For specific histologies, additional imaging may be needed [EXTSARC-1, SARC-A].
Assess for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, including social determinants of health [EXTSARC-1 footnote e].

Additional imaging as indicated per Principles of Imaging (SARC-A). For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas, biopsy may be omitted if imaging is pathognomonic (e.g., WDLPS) by expert radiologist within expert multidisciplinary tumor board, and no preoperative treatment is planned [RETSARC-1 footnote d].

Genetic counseling and testing for inherited cancer predisposition syndromes: Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53), neurofibromatosis (NF1), hereditary retinoblastoma (RB1), Lynch syndrome, FAP/desmoid tumors (APC), Carney-Stratakis syndrome (SDH subunits). Also for patients with personal/family history suggestive of other predisposition syndromes [EXTSARC-1, RETSARC-1, DESM-1, SARC-H].

FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) for staging, prognostication, grading, determining response to therapy, especially for certain histologies (e.g., RMS, to differentiate neurofibroma vs. MPNST in neurofibromatosis, for WDLPS vs. ddLPS) [SARC-A, RMS-1 footnote b].
MRI of total spine for myxoid/round cell liposarcoma due to propensity for spine metastases [SARC-A 2 of 5].
CNS MRI (or CT) for alveolar soft part sarcoma, angiosarcoma, left-sided cardiac sarcoma [SARC-A 1 of 5].
Evaluation for Gardner syndrome/FAP in patients with desmoid tumors [DESM-1].

For phyllodes tumors: breast MRI with and without contrast; additional imaging (e.g., CT) of primary tumor as necessary for surgical planning. Genetic counseling for breast and sarcoma genes plus other inherited cancer genes consistent with family phenotype [PHYLLSARC-1, PHYLLSARC-2].

For malignant phyllodes tumor: CT chest (consider CT abdomen/pelvis) with contrast; consider additional imaging (whole body FDG-PET/CT, bone scan, brain MRI) as clinically indicated given propensity for lung/bone/visceral metastases [PHYLLSARC-2, footnote e].

Patients with neurofibromatosis: at risk for multiple sarcomas at various locations; consider FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) for initial workup to differentiate between neurofibroma(s) and MPNST [EXTSARC-1 footnote f].
DFSP with fibrosarcomatous changes and/or malignant transformation should be treated per algorithms for other soft tissue sarcomas [EXTSARC-1 footnote h].
For RMS: FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) may be useful for initial staging due to possibility of nodal metastases and unusual sites of initial metastatic disease in adults [RMS-1 footnote b].
For malignant phyllodes tumor: consider symptom-driven brain MRI with and without contrast for 5 years [PHYLLSARC-3 footnote n].

surgery.role: Surgical resection with oncologically appropriate negative margins is the standard primary treatment for most patients with STS. The goal of surgery is functional limb preservation for extremity sarcomas when possible, and complete tumor removal for all sites. Surgery is often combined with RT and/or systemic therapy to improve local control and reduce recurrence risk. For selected low-grade tumors, surgery alone may be definitive. For desmoid tumors, surgery is less preferred and reserved for specific situations. For phyllodes tumors, surgery with ≥1 cm margins is the cornerstone of treatment.
principles: The multidisciplinary surgical team should include expertise in the management of sarcomas at specific anatomic sites as well as reconstruction [SARC-D 1 of 4].

Pretreatment pathologic diagnosis (histologic subtype and grade) is almost always necessary for optimal treatment planning, including surgical margin planning and discussion of neoadjuvant therapy [SARC-D 1 of 4].
