Percutaneous core needle biopsy is preferred; the biopsy tract should avoid potential tumor contamination of uninvolved compartments and be in line with future surgical resection incision. In certain situations, MRI and FDG-PET/CT imaging prior to biopsy may allow targeting of enhancing/metabolically active components [SARC-D 1 of 4].

There is no clarity in the literature on when sentinel node staging in STS should be done; multidisciplinary discussion should occur regarding its role in certain histologies (clear cell, angiosarcoma, epithelioid, alveolar RMS) [SARC-D 1 of 4].

Ideal surgical procedure resects tumor with pathologically negative resection margins. However, planned close margins or microscopically positive margins may be appropriate to preserve critical structures (major vessels, nerves, bones, joints), especially in multimodality therapy [SARC-D 1 of 4].

For extremity sarcomas, the goal should be functional limb preservation within the realm of appropriate oncologic resection ('limb-sparing surgery'). Radical excision/entire anatomic compartment resection is not routinely necessary [SARC-D 1 of 4].

Prior to considering amputation, patients should be evaluated by a surgeon with expertise in the treatment of soft tissue sarcomas. Amputation should be considered for patient preference or if gross total resection would render the limb nonfunctional [SARC-D 1 of 4].

For patients with previous unplanned excision: staging imaging of primary tumor site determines optimal management. For gross residual tumor, follow EXTSARC-1. For no gross residual tumor but nononcologic margins, manage as 'Failure to Obtain Oncologically Appropriate Margins' [SARC-D 1-2 of 4].
For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas, unplanned spillage of microscopic tumor into the free intra-abdominal cavity is associated with increased risk for future sarcomatosis [SARC-D 2 of 4].

Surgical technique: ideally, prior surgical or biopsy site contaminated with microscopic tumor should be excised en bloc with definitive resection if technically possible with acceptable morbidity. Dissection through grossly normal tissue planes. Major vessels/nerves do not need resection if adventitia/perineurium removed and underlying structures not involved with gross tumor [SARC-D 2 of 4].

For certain highly infiltrative histologies (myxofibrosarcoma, DFSP), staged resections with temporary wound coverage to obtain negative pathologic margins prior to definitive closure/reconstruction can reduce local recurrence risk [SARC-D 2 of 4].

Prehabilitation and rehabilitation: when significant postoperative dysfunction is anticipated (extremity amputation, major limb-sparing, truncal/intra-abdominal resections), strong consideration should be given to outpatient prehabilitation and rehabilitation to optimize functional outcomes and quality of life [SARC-D 3 of 4].

Prior to amputation or major limb-sparing surgery, formal PM&R consultation should be offered. Multimodality prehabilitation (exercise, nutritional supplementation, anxiety-coping) is associated with greater improvements in functional capacity and lower risk for postoperative complications following major abdominal surgery [SARC-D 3 of 4].

Rehabilitation plan should consider oncology treatment-related side effects (lymphedema, neuropathy, fatigue, radiation fibrosis, impaired bone healing). Pain management integrated early, especially for neuropathic pain or phantom limb pain. Psychological concerns addressed with referrals to mental health professionals and peer support groups [SARC-D 3-4 of 4].

procedures.name: Limb-sparing surgery (wide local excision)
procedures.approach: En bloc resection of tumor with a cuff of normal tissue. Goal is negative margins while preserving functional limb. For extremity STS, includes resection of biopsy tract and any contaminated structures. May require reconstruction by plastic, vascular, or thoracic surgeons. Adjuvant RT commonly used.
procedures.indications: Primary management for most extremity and truncal STS. Preferred over amputation when oncologically appropriate margins can be achieved with acceptable functional outcome.
procedures.key_complications: Wound healing complications (higher with preoperative RT, especially lower extremity; 35% vs 17% postoperative RT in randomized trial [SARC-E 1 of 6]), fibrosis, edema, joint stiffness, lymphedema, nerve injury, vascular compromise, functional deficit.

procedures.name: Amputation
procedures.approach: Radical resection through the joint proximal to the tumor. May include hemipelvectomy, hip disarticulation, above-knee, below-knee, or upper extremity amputations. Prior to amputation, patient should be evaluated by a sarcoma surgeon and PM&R.
procedures.indications: Patient preference; or when gross total resection with limb-sparing surgery would render limb nonfunctional; or for recurrent disease not amenable to limb-sparing options. Amputation may improve local control in patients not candidates for limb-sparing surgery [SARC-D 1 of 4].
procedures.key_complications: Phantom limb pain, neuropathic pain, psychological impact, functional disability, need for prosthetic rehabilitation, wound complications, infection.

procedures.name: Wide resection for retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma
procedures.approach: Complete gross resection with en bloc removal of involved organs if necessary. Compartmental resection (including involved viscera, muscle, peritoneum) may improve local control. IORT can be considered for positive margins.
procedures.indications: Primary treatment for resectable retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal STS. Surgery often results in physical disability affecting major organ systems (cardiopulmonary, nutrition, performance status) [SARC-D 3 of 4].
procedures.key_complications: Bowel injury, vascular injury, ureteral injury, pancreatic leak, hemorrhage, infection, incisional hernia, organ dysfunction, adhesions, bowel obstruction.

procedures.name: Surgery for desmoid tumors
procedures.approach: Wide excision with intention of negative margins. However, R1 margins are acceptable if achieving R0 would produce excessive morbidity. For intra-abdominal/retroperitoneal desmoids, surgery is less preferred and should only be considered if agreed upon by multidisciplinary tumor board [DESM-4 footnote h, i].
procedures.indications: Not first-line except in certain situations. May be considered for progressive, morbid, or symptomatic desmoid tumors when other therapies have failed or are not appropriate. Surgery often results in physical disability.
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence (even with negative margins, recurrence rates high), functional morbidity, wound complications, nerve injury. For intra-abdominal: bowel obstruction, fistula, infection.

procedures.name: Surgery for phyllodes tumors (borderline/malignant)
procedures.approach: Wide excision with intention of obtaining surgical margins ≥1 cm. Mastectomy may be needed to achieve adequate margins. Axillary staging is not performed. For malignant phyllodes, expedite initial excision without delay for staging scans due to rapid growth [PHYLLSARC-2].
procedures.indications: Primary treatment for localized borderline/malignant phyllodes tumors. Due to high local recurrence rates with narrow/positive margins, attempts at nipple/skin sparing approach close to tumor is discouraged [PHYLLSARC-2A footnote k].
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence, wound complications, chest wall deformity, cosmetic deformity (especially with mastectomy).

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined by the ability to achieve gross total removal of tumor with oncologically appropriate margins while preserving critical structures and function. For extremity STS: tumor must be amenable to limb-sparing surgery or amputation with acceptable functional outcome. For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal: tumor should not involve vital structures in a manner that precludes safe complete resection. Unresectable tumors are those involving vital structures whose removal would cause unacceptable morbidity, or patients medically unfit for major resection. For desmoid tumors: resectability is considered but surgery is less preferred due to high recurrence rates and potential morbidity. For phyllodes tumors: resectability requires ability to obtain ≥1 cm margins; borderline resectable tumors may be considered for preoperative RT [PHYLLSARC-2A footnote f]. In the rare situation where surgery is not an option, consider observation [EXTSARC-2 footnote l].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy is a critical component of multimodality treatment for STS, primarily to improve local control when combined with surgery. It can be administered as neoadjuvant (preoperative), adjuvant (postoperative), definitive (for unresectable disease), or palliative treatment. The panel has expressed a general preference for preoperative over postoperative radiotherapy for extremity/body wall/head and neck STS due to lower total dose, smaller field sizes, and potential for reduced late toxicities, despite higher acute wound healing complications [SARC-E 1 of 6]. For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal STS, neoadjuvant RT can be considered in selected high-risk patients, but adjuvant RT is discouraged routinely [SARC-E 3 of 6]. SBRT is used for metastatic disease and select primary tumors. Palliative RT improves symptoms from unresectable or metastatic disease.
principles: Radiation should be delivered using the most appropriate technique to cover target volume while maintaining dose constraints to normal tissues. Techniques include IMRT (preferred), 3D conformal RT, electron beam therapy, or proton beam therapy in challenging anatomic locations. Daily image guidance is essential to ensure adequate targeting and sparing of adjacent normal tissue [SARC-E 1 of 6].

For extremity/body wall/head and neck: Total RT doses are always determined based on tissue tolerance. Neoadjuvant: 50-50.4 Gy (1.8-2 Gy/fraction). Adjuvant: 50-50.4 Gy to large volume followed by boost (10-16 Gy for negative margins, 16-18 Gy for R1, 20-26 Gy for R2). Brachytherapy can be used alone or with EBRT [SARC-E 1-2 of 6].
For myxoid liposarcoma, a dose reduction to 36 Gy can be considered for preoperative RT [SARC-E 1 of 6].

Conventionally fractionated RT remains standard. Moderately hypofractionated preoperative regimens have demonstrated acceptable early local control but long-term data not yet available; may be appropriate for select patients unable to receive standard course [SARC-E 1 of 6].

For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal: When EBRT used, IMRT/IGRT/proton beam therapy should be used to improve therapeutic ratio. Neoadjuvant doses: 50-50.4 Gy (1.8-2 Gy/fraction). Consider IORT boost for known/suspected positive margins (10-12.5 Gy for microscopic, 15 Gy for gross) [SARC-E 3 of 6].

Alternative approach for retroperitoneal: 45-50 Gy in 25-28 fractions to entire CTV with dose-painted SIB to total 57.5 Gy in 25 fractions to high-risk retroperitoneal margin jointly defined by surgeon and radiation oncologist (no boost after surgery) [SARC-E 3 of 6].

Adjuvant RT following surgery is discouraged for retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma. If RT not given prior to surgery, consider follow-up with possible neoadjuvant RT at time of localized recurrence [SARC-E 3 of 6].

For desmoid tumors: Definitive RT is an appropriate option primarily for non-mesenteric desmoid tumors, often reserved for patients who cannot tolerate or progress through systemic therapy and where surgery would be too morbid or result in positive margins. Recommended dose: 56 Gy in 28 fractions. Treat similarly to high-grade STS given infiltrative nature [SARC-E 2 of 6].

For borderline/malignant phyllodes: General sarcoma dose guidelines utilized: 50-60 Gy in 1.8-2 Gy/fraction postoperatively. No data supporting hypofractionation. Timing of pre- vs postoperative RT determined in tandem with surgeon and multidisciplinary team based on tumor size, proximity to skin/chest wall, planned surgery extent, and reconstructive considerations [SARC-E 3 of 6].

SBRT: Delivers ablative doses to tumors using specialized immobilization, treatment planning, and image guidance. Commonly used for metastatic sarcomas involving lungs, liver, bone, lymph nodes, and select primary tumors. Dose/fractionation determined by experienced radiation oncologist based on normal tissue constraints. Patient selection: oligometastatic disease, oligoprogressive disease with other sites stable, or treatment within previously irradiated field [SARC-E 4 of 6].

Palliative RT: Dose/fractionation based on normal tissue constraints, performance status, patient prognosis, and prior treatment history. Patients with limited volume metastatic disease and excellent performance status may benefit from dose escalation through IMRT or SBRT. Prior irradiation to treatment site may benefit from IMRT or SBRT for safety of reirradiation [SARC-E 4 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Neoadjuvant RT (extremity/body wall/head/neck)
dose_frameworks.indication: Preferred approach for stage II-III STS. For myxoid liposarcoma, 36 Gy may be considered [SARC-E 1 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT (extremity/body wall/head/neck) – initial volume
dose_frameworks.schedule: Once daily, postoperative after wound healing
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients who did not receive preoperative RT. Final dose determined by margin status: 60 Gy for R0, 66 Gy for R1 after boost [SARC-E 2 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT boost (extremity/body wall/head/neck) – R0 margins
dose_frameworks.indication: For negative margins (final 60-66 Gy depending on margin). Boost may not be necessary for widely negative margins [SARC-E 2 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT boost – R1 margins
dose_frameworks.indication: For microscopically positive margins (final dose 66-68 Gy) [SARC-E 2 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT boost – R2 margins
dose_frameworks.indication: For gross residual disease (final dose 70-76 Gy) [SARC-E 2 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT for unresectable disease
dose_frameworks.schedule: Once daily, or alternative hypofractionated regimens
dose_frameworks.indication: For nonsurgical candidates with unresectable primary or metastatic disease [SARC-E 2 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Neoadjuvant RT (retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Once daily, preoperative. Consider IORT boost intraoperatively.
dose_frameworks.indication: Selected patients at high risk for local recurrence [SARC-E 3 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Alternative neoadjuvant RT (retroperitoneal) – dose-painted SIB
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 45-50 Gy to CTV; 57.5 Gy SIB to high-risk margin
dose_frameworks.schedule: Once daily, preoperative. No boost after surgery. To be used in experienced centers only [SARC-E 3 of 6].
dose_frameworks.indication: Alternative approach for retroperitoneal sarcoma. High-risk margins jointly defined by surgeon and radiation oncologist [SARC-E 3 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT for desmoid tumors
dose_frameworks.indication: For non-mesenteric desmoid tumors where surgery would be too morbid or would result in positive margins [SARC-E 2 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Postoperative RT for phyllodes tumors
dose_frameworks.indication: For malignant phyllodes tumors after breast conservation surgery (adjuvant RT recommended regardless of margin status); for high-risk features after mastectomy. Conventionally fractionated whole breast/post-mastectomy radiation recommended [SARC-E 3 of 6, PHYLLSARC-2A footnote i].

approaches.name: Preoperative (Neoadjuvant) RT – Extremity/Body Wall/Head/Neck
approaches.dose_fractionation: 50-50.4 Gy in 25-28 fractions (1.8-2 Gy/fraction). Dose reduction to 36 Gy for myxoid liposarcoma. Hypofractionated regimens (e.g., 5 fractions) may be considered for select patients [SARC-E 1 of 6].
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not typically used with RT alone. However, for select histologies (UPS-related sarcomas), neoadjuvant pembrolizumab with RT followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab is an option [SARC-G 1 of 14].
approaches.indication: Category 1 recommendation for stage II-III STS. Preferred over postoperative RT due to lower total dose, smaller fields, and reduced late toxicities. Particularly useful when negative margins are anticipated and for large tumors [SARC-E 1 of 6].
approaches.key_trial: Canadian Sarcoma Group randomized trial demonstrated similar local control and PFS but greater acute wound complications (35% vs 17%) for preop vs postop RT [SARC-E 1 of 6]. RTOG 0630 showed reduced late toxicity with image-guided RT [SARC-E 1 of 6].
approaches.toxicities: Acute wound healing complications (especially lower extremity), fatigue, skin reaction, edema. Late: fibrosis, joint stiffness, edema, lymphedema, bone necrosis, secondary malignancy.

approaches.name: Postoperative (Adjuvant) RT – Extremity/Body Wall/Head/Neck
approaches.dose_fractionation: Initial 50-50.4 Gy in 25-28 fractions to large volume, followed by boost to tumor bed (10-16 Gy for R0, 16-18 Gy for R1, 20-26 Gy for R2). Total dose 60-70 Gy. Alternative: brachytherapy alone (45 Gy LDR or 36 Gy HDR for negative margins) or with EBRT boost [SARC-E 2 of 6].
approaches.indication: For patients who did not receive preoperative RT, especially when margins are close or positive. Category 1 recommendation. Potential benefits: ability to review final pathology and margin status, lower wound complication rates [SARC-E 2 of 6].
approaches.key_trial: Randomized study by Yang et al showed significant reduction in 10-year local recurrence with postoperative RT in high-grade lesions (0% vs 22%) and low-grade (5% vs 32%) [MS-14].
approaches.toxicities: Higher late toxicity compared to preoperative: fibrosis, edema, joint stiffness due to larger fields and higher doses. Intervals beyond 8 weeks between resection and RT are not recommended [MS-8].

approaches.name: Definitive RT for Unresectable Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: At least 63 Gy, up to 70-80 Gy based on normal tissue tolerance. Alternative hypofractionated regimens may be considered [SARC-E 2 of 6].
approaches.indication: Nonsurgical candidates with unresectable primary or metastatic disease. Long-term local control may be inferior to multimodality therapy [SARC-E 2 of 6].
approaches.key_trial: Single-institution study showed local control 51% for tumors <5 cm vs 9% for >10 cm; better outcomes with ≥63 Gy [MS-19].
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, skin reaction, fibrosis, edema, potential damage to adjacent organs depending on location.

approaches.name: Neoadjuvant RT for Retroperitoneal/Intra-Abdominal Sarcoma
approaches.dose_fractionation: 50-50.4 Gy in 25-28 fractions (1.8-2 Gy/fraction). Consider IORT boost: 10-12.5 Gy for microscopic positive margin, 15 Gy for gross disease. Alternative: 45-50 Gy in 25-28 fractions with dose-painted SIB to 57.5 Gy in 25 fractions (experienced centers only) [SARC-E 3 of 6].
approaches.indication: Selected patients at high risk for local recurrence. IMRT/IGRT/proton therapy recommended to improve therapeutic ratio [SARC-E 3 of 6].
approaches.key_trial: EORTC-62092 (STRASS) phase III trial: neoadjuvant RT + surgery vs surgery alone for primary retroperitoneal sarcoma. Primary endpoint not met (median abdominal RFS 4.5 vs 5 years, HR 1.01). Post-hoc analysis suggested benefit for liposarcoma [MS-23].
approaches.toxicities: Lymphopenia (77%), anemia (12%), hypoalbuminemia (12%) in STRASS trial. Potential bowel toxicity. Adjuvant RT discouraged [SARC-E 3 of 6].

approaches.name: SBRT for Metastases / Select Primary Tumors
approaches.dose_fractionation: Variable; determined by experienced radiation oncologist based on normal tissue constraints. Commonly used for lung, liver, bone, lymph node metastases. Dose/fractionation: e.g., 30-60 Gy in 3-5 fractions for lung metastases [SARC-E 4 of 6].
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: May be used sequentially with systemic therapy.
approaches.indication: Patients with oligometastatic disease, oligoprogressive disease with other sites stable/controlled, or treatment within previously irradiated field [SARC-E 4 of 6].
approaches.key_trial: Retrospective studies: high rates of local control with favorable toxicity for sarcoma pulmonary metastases (Dhakal et al, Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012) [SARC-E 6 of 6].
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, pneumonitis (lung), hepatitis (liver), bone pain flare, rib fracture, radiation necrosis (brain). Low rates of grade 3+ toxicity in reported series.

approaches.name: Palliative RT
approaches.dose_fractionation: Determined based on normal tissue constraints, performance status, prognosis, prior treatment history. Hypofractionated regimens common (e.g., 30 Gy in 10 fractions, 20 Gy in 5 fractions, 8 Gy single fraction for bone metastases). Patients with limited volume and excellent performance status may benefit from dose escalation with IMRT or SBRT [SARC-E 4 of 6].
approaches.indication: Symptomatic unresectable or metastatic disease: pain, bleeding, obstruction, neurologic compromise.
approaches.key_trial: Owen D, et al. Pract Radiat Oncol 2025;15:e388-e395 [EXTSARC-6 footnote ee].
approaches.toxicities: Acute: fatigue, skin reaction, nausea, diarrhea (depending on site). Late: fibrosis, fistula, necrosis, secondary malignancy.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for soft tissue sarcoma is tailored by histologic subtype, stage, and treatment setting (neoadjuvant/adjuvant, first-line advanced/metastatic, subsequent lines). Anthracycline-based regimens are the backbone for most subtypes. The NCCN guideline provides extensive histology-specific recommendations [SARC-G 1-7 of 14]. Neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy is considered for high-risk patients to improve local control and reduce distant recurrence. For advanced/metastatic disease, first-line options include doxorubicin, epirubicin, liposomal doxorubicin, and combination regimens (AIM, AD, epirubicin/ifosfamide). Targeted agents (pazopanib, eribulin, trabectedin, TKIs) and immunotherapies (pembrolizumab, nivolumab ± ipilimumab) are used in subsequent lines or for specific molecular subtypes (NTRK fusions, TMB-H, MSI-H). Dexrazoxane may be added as cardioprotectant for high-dose anthracyclines (doxorubicin >250 mg/m2) [SARC-G 1 of 14 footnote d]. The panel emphasizes that patients should be managed by a multidisciplinary team with sarcoma expertise prior to initiating therapy.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: AIM (Doxorubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna), Epirubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna, Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab with RT followed by adjuvant Pembrolizumab for UPS-related sarcomas (limb girdle/extremity UPS/MFS/ddLPS/pleomorphic liposarcoma) [SARC-G 1 of 14].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Anthracycline-based regimens are preferred. For UPS-related sarcomas, pembrolizumab with RT showed improved outcomes in SU2C-SARC032 trial [SARC-G 1 of 14 reference 6].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: AD (Doxorubicin/Dacarbazine) for LMS or if ifosfamide not appropriate; Doxorubicin alone; Ifosfamide/Mesna; Trabectedin (for myxoid liposarcoma); Docetaxel/Gemcitabine (category 2B) [SARC-G 1 of 14].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Anthracycline-based: Doxorubicin, Epirubicin, Liposomal Doxorubicin, AD (Doxorubicin/Dacarbazine), AIM (Doxorubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna), Epirubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna, Doxorubicin/Trabectedin (for LMS), NTRK inhibitors (Larotrectinib, Entrectinib, Repotrectinib) for NTRK fusion-positive sarcomas regardless of subtype [SARC-G 1 of 14].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Doxorubicin remains standard first-line. Combination therapy (AIM) improves response rate and PFS but not OS. For LMS, doxorubicin/trabectedin showed benefit in LMS-04 trial [SARC-G 1 of 14 reference 14]. NTRK inhibitors demonstrate high response rates in NTRK fusion-positive tumors.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Gemcitabine; Docetaxel/Gemcitabine (category 2B); Pazopanib (for patients ineligible for IV therapy or not candidates for anthracyclines); Selpercatinib (for RET fusion-positive tumors); Dacarbazine/Gemcitabine (category 2B) [SARC-G 1 of 14].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Subsequent Lines of Therapy for Advanced/Metastatic Disease
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Anthracycline-based (if not previously used, with attention to lifetime doxorubicin dose), Pazopanib (category 1 for liposarcoma and LMS, category 2A for other subtypes); Trabectedin (category 1 for liposarcoma and LMS, category 2A for other subtypes); Docetaxel/Gemcitabine; NTRK inhibitors (repotrectinib if not previously given) [SARC-G 1 of 14].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Pazopanib approved for non-lipogenic STS based on PALETTE trial (PFS 4.6 vs 1.6 months). Eribulin improved OS in LPS. Trabectedin demonstrated benefit in L-type sarcomas. Immunotherapy reserved for select subtypes (UPS, MFS, ddLPS, cutaneous angiosarcoma) or TMB-H/MSI-H tumors.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Dacarbazine; Ifosfamide/Mesna; Temozolomide; Vinorelbine; Regorafenib; Gemcitabine; Dacarbazine/Gemcitabine; Gemcitabine/Vinorelbine (category 2B); Gemcitabine/Pazopanib (category 2B); Pembrolizumab or nivolumab ± ipilimumab (for MFS, UPS, ddLPS, cutaneous angiosarcoma, TMB-H, MSI-H); Cabozantinib (category 2B); Afamitresgene Autoleucel (for HLA-A*02 positive synovial sarcoma with MAGE-A4) [SARC-G 1 of 14].

key_regimens.name: AIM (Doxorubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin), hemorrhagic cystitis (ifosfamide), nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, fatigue, nausea/vomiting, alopecia

key_regimens.name: AD (Doxorubicin/Dacarbazine)
key_regimens.setting: Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant; First-line Advanced/Metastatic (for LMS or if ifosfamide not appropriate)

key_regimens.name: Pazopanib
key_regimens.setting: First-line (for patients ineligible for IV therapy or not anthracycline candidates); Subsequent lines for advanced/metastatic non-adipocytic STS
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, hypertension, diarrhea, hepatotoxicity, hypothyroidism, nausea, hand-foot skin reaction, hemorrhage, thromboembolism

key_regimens.name: Eribulin
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent lines – category 1 for liposarcoma, category 2A for other subtypes
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression (neutropenia), peripheral neuropathy, fatigue, alopecia, nausea

key_regimens.name: Trabectedin
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent lines – category 1 for liposarcoma and LMS, category 2A for other subtypes
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression (neutropenia), hepatotoxicity, fatigue, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, rhabdomyolysis, cardiotoxicity (rare)

key_regimens.name: Docetaxel/Gemcitabine
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, fatigue, fluid retention (docetaxel), rash, pulmonary toxicity (rare), peripheral edema

key_regimens.name: Nirogacestat (for Desmoid Tumors)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, nausea, diarrhea, liver enzyme elevation, hypophosphatemia, ovarian dysfunction

key_regimens.name: VAC (Vincristine/Dactinomycin/Cyclophosphamide) – for Non-Pleomorphic RMS
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, neurotoxicity (vincristine), hepatotoxicity (dactinomycin), hemorrhagic cystitis (cyclophosphamide), nausea/vomiting, alopecia, secondary leukemia

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent lines for advanced/metastatic: MFS, UPS, ddLPS, cutaneous angiosarcoma, undifferentiated sarcomas, TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb), MSI-H/dMMR tumors regardless of subtype

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks (200 mg) or every 6 weeks (400 mg)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, hypophysitis), dermatitis, nephritis, fatigue. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab with different dosing/administration [SARC-G 8 of 14 footnote u].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Principles of Treatment Response Assessment and Follow-Up
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Assessment of treatment response is tailored to disease stage, histology, and treatment modality. For neoadjuvant therapy, response evaluation is performed during and after preoperative chemotherapy or radiation using imaging, with FDG-PET/CT optional to determine histopathologic response. For advanced/metastatic disease, response is assessed every 2-3 cycles of systemic therapy (typically every 6-12 weeks) using RECIST criteria or FDG-PET/CT. For unresectable disease treated with definitive RT or systemic therapy, follow-up imaging is recommended to assess treatment response. For patients undergoing preoperative RT, a 3- to 6-week interval is necessary before resection to allow acute reactions to subside [MS-7]. After completion of primary treatment (surgery ± RT ± systemic therapy), surveillance follows a risk-stratified schedule based on stage and histology.
response_logic: FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) may be useful in determining response to neoadjuvant systemic therapy. A reduction in SUVmax of ≥40% after preoperative chemotherapy is associated with significantly lower risk of recurrence and death after complete resection and postoperative RT [EXTSARC-3 footnote w, MS-12-13].
A 35% reduction in SUV after the first cycle of chemotherapy was a sensitive predictor of histopathologic response in a prospective study of 50 patients with resectable high-grade STS [MS-12-13].

For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal STS: patients whose tumors become resectable following primary treatment (systemic therapy and/or RT) should be managed as described for resectable disease [RETSARC-4].

For desmoid tumors: response to therapy is assessed with imaging. Spontaneous regression has been reported in up to 20% of patients. Response to RT is slow, with continuing regression seen even after 3 years [MS-33].

For phyllodes tumors: rapid growth pattern requires expedited evaluation. After neoadjuvant RT, surgical re-evaluation should occur during and immediately after preoperative RT to reassess resectability [PHYLLSARC-2A footnote f].

For patients with metastatic disease, follow-up imaging including chest and other known sites of disease should be performed every 2-6 months for 2-3 years, then every 6 months to complete a total of 5 years, then annually [EXTSARC-5, SARC-A 3-4 of 5].
imaging_recommendations: Contrasted cross-sectional imaging with MRI (with and without contrast) is preferred over CT for primary site evaluation. CT is preferred for retroperitoneum. For long-term survivors, chest x-rays and nonionizing imaging modalities may be considered to minimize radiation exposure [SARC-A 1 of 5].

Chest imaging: CT chest without contrast is preferred for detection of pulmonary metastases. For some histologies (e.g., WDLPS), routine chest imaging is not necessary [ALT/WDLPS-1 footnote d, SARC-A 2 of 5].

Surveillance schedule by stage: Stage I: H&P every 3-6 months for 3 years, then annually. Consider chest imaging every 6-12 months. Periodic imaging of primary site based on risk of locoregional recurrence. Stage II/III and synchronous Stage IV: H&P and imaging of chest and other known sites of metastatic disease every 2-6 months for 2-3 years, then every 6 months to complete 5 years, then annually. Postoperative baseline imaging of primary site recommended [SARC-A 3-4 of 5].

Retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal STS: follow-up imaging (chest/abdomen/pelvis CT preferred or MRI) every 3-6 months for 3 years, then every 6 months to complete 5 years, then annually. For well-differentiated liposarcoma, routine chest imaging is not necessary [SARC-A 4 of 5, RETSARC-3 footnote r].

Desmoid tumors: imaging every 3-6 months for 3 years, then every 6-12 months thereafter. MRI is preferred. Ultrasound may be considered for select locations (e.g., abdominal wall) for long-term follow-up, done by an ultrasonographer experienced in musculoskeletal disease [SARC-A 5 of 5].

Phyllodes tumors (malignant): H&P and imaging (CT C/A/P and breast/chest MRI with and without contrast) every 3-6 months for 3 years, then every 6 months to complete 5 years, then annually. Consider symptom-driven brain MRI for 5 years [PHYLLSARC-3].
After 10 years, the likelihood of developing a recurrence is small and follow-up should be individualized [EXTSARC-3A footnote o].
In situations where the area is easily followed by physical examination, imaging may not be required [EXTSARC-3A footnote n].
Ultrasound may be used to detect early local recurrence and small nodules <0.5 cm; MRI should be used if ultrasound results are inconclusive [MS-20-21].

Specific histologies require additional sites: ASPS (CNS MRI, bone scan); angiosarcoma (CNS MRI, regional lymph node basin imaging); myxoid/round cell liposarcoma (total spine MRI, WB MRI for soft tissues); leiomyosarcoma (abdomen/pelvis, bone scan); epithelioid sarcoma (abdomen/pelvis, regional nodes) [SARC-A 1-2 of 5].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If local recurrence is suspected during follow-up, biopsy should be performed to confirm diagnosis. For patients with previous unplanned excision, staging imaging determines optimal management [SARC-D 1 of 4].

For patients with a local recurrence, treatment decisions should be made using the same algorithm as for a new primary lesion. If LR can be excised, decision regarding re-irradiation should be made on a case-by-case basis [EXTSARC-6 footnote ff].

For recurrent disease (metastatic): If oligometastatic, local therapies (metastasectomy, SBRT, ablation, embolization) can be considered. For disseminated disease, systemic therapy, palliative RT, or observation (if asymptomatic) are options [EXTSARC-6, RETSARC-6].

For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal recurrence: biopsy should be considered if diagnosis is not clinically definitive. Surgery to obtain oncologically appropriate margins is recommended if resectable. Consider neoadjuvant therapy (RT if not previously given, systemic therapy) for selected cases [RETSARC-6].

For desmoid tumors: disease progression on imaging (with or without symptoms) may trigger active therapy. Continuation of observation is appropriate for minimal symptoms and non-critical location [DESM-2, DESM-3].

For malignant phyllodes: local recurrence is managed per initial treatment pathways. Oligometastatic disease: consider systemic therapy for all patients, metastasectomy (shown to improve OS in retrospective study: HR 0.33; 95% CI 0.14-0.78), RT/SBRT, ablation, embolization [PHYLLSARC-5, PHYLLSARC-5A footnote q].

prognosis.overall: Soft tissue sarcomas (STS) are rare, accounting for approximately 1% of adult malignancies. In 2022, an estimated 13,190 new cases and 5,130 deaths were projected in the US [Siegel 2022]. Prognosis depends critically on histologic grade, tumor size, depth, site, margin status, and subtype. High-grade tumors have significantly worse outcomes; the FNCLCC grading system (based on differentiation, mitotic count, and necrosis) is the standard three-tier system used for risk stratification [ST-5]. Retroperitoneal sarcomas generally have poorer prognosis than extremity sarcomas due to late presentation and difficulty achieving negative margins. Desmoid tumors (aggressive fibromatosis) are non-metastasizing but can cause significant local morbidity; spontaneous regression occurs in up to 20% of patients [DESM-2]. Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) in adults has a worse prognosis than in children: 5-year overall survival (OS) rates of 27% in adults versus 63% in children [Sultan 2009]. For pleomorphic RMS, 5-year OS in adults is approximately 40% [Ferrari 2003]. Advanced/metastatic STS has a poor disease-free interval; median survival is on the order of 12–18 months. For localized STS treated with curative intent, reported 5-year OS varies widely: for high-risk extremity STS, the SMAC meta-analysis reported 5-year OS of 64–65% [SMAC 1997]; the Italian cooperative trial showed 66% vs 46% (p=0.04) with adjuvant chemotherapy [Frustaci 2001]; and the MAID chemoradiation series reported 87% vs 58% (p=0.0003) compared with historical controls [DeLaney 2003]. In the RTOG 9514 trial of neoadjuvant chemoradiation, 5-year OS was 71% [Kraybill 2010]. For retroperitoneal STS, the STRASS trial reported no significant difference in abdominal recurrence-free survival between neoadjuvant RT plus surgery vs surgery alone (HR 1.01, p=0.95) [Bonvalot 2020], underscoring the need for individualized risk assessment.
prognostic_factors: Anatomic site (extremity vs retroperitoneal vs head/neck)
Histologic subtype (e.g., alveolar soft part sarcoma, synovial sarcoma, dedifferentiated liposarcoma, angiosarcoma)
Presence of distant metastasis at diagnosis (stage IV)
Response to neoadjuvant therapy (e.g., pathologic response, FDG-PET SUV change)
Genetic alterations (e.g., PAX3-FOXO1 vs PAX7-FOXO1 in alveolar RMS, CTNNB1 mutation in desmoids)
Molecular subtype (e.g., SS18-SSX fusion type in synovial sarcoma)
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis in soft tissue sarcoma is highly heterogeneous. The AJCC 8th edition staging system (based on T, N, M, and histologic grade) provides anatomic stage/prognostic groups for trunk/extremity and retroperitoneal sarcomas, but not for head/neck or abdomen/thoracic visceral organs (new classification needs data collection) [ST-4, ST-5, ST-6, ST-7]. The NCCN guidelines emphasize that treatment decisions must be made by a multidisciplinary team with sarcoma expertise. For desmoid tumors, the primary concern is local recurrence rather than metastasis; both CTNNB1 mutation status and tumor location influence recurrence risk. For RMS, risk stratification (e.g., Oberlin risk factors) is critical for therapy planning. The overall survival and recurrence rates cited above derive from clinical trials and meta-analyses, but real-world outcomes may differ. The panel notes that adherence to NCCN guideline–concordant treatment is associated with improved survival [Voss 2017].

disease_related.management: Re-resection if feasible; consider RT (if not previously given) or re-irradiation with brachytherapy/IMRT/proton therapy; systemic therapy for high-risk or unresectable recurrences.

disease_related.complication: Distant metastasis (most commonly lung; also liver, bone, peritoneum, soft tissues, spine depending on histology)
disease_related.management: Metastasectomy for oligometastatic disease (e.g., pulmonary resection); SBRT for selected lung/liver/bone metastases; ablation/embolization for liver metastases; systemic therapy for disseminated disease.

disease_related.complication: Lymph node metastasis (e.g., in angiosarcoma, clear cell sarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma, alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma)
disease_related.management: Regional lymph node dissection; consider RT ± systemic therapy.

disease_related.complication: Functional morbidity from primary tumor (e.g., limb dysfunction, pain, bleeding, infection)
disease_related.management: Multimodality management including surgery, RT, systemic therapy; prehabilitation and rehabilitation; palliative interventions for fungating/necrotic tumors.

chemotherapy: Cardiotoxicity from anthracyclines (doxorubicin, epirubicin); dexrazoxane may be added if doxorubicin >250 mg/m² [SARC-G 8 of 14].
Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia); growth factor support may be used.
Nephrotoxicity and hemorrhagic cystitis from ifosfamide; mesna prophylaxis is recommended.
Peripheral neuropathy from docetaxel, vincristine, and eribulin.
Fatigue, nausea, alopecia, and mucositis are common.
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) with checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab, atezolizumab): dermatitis, colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, etc.
Hypersensitivity/infusion reactions; subcutaneous formulations available for some agents (e.g., pembrolizumab + hyaluronidase, atezolizumab + hyaluronidase, nivolumab + hyaluronidase).
targeted_therapy: Pazopanib: hypertension, diarrhea, hepatotoxicity, fatigue, hypothyroidism, hand-foot skin reaction.
Trabectedin: transaminitis, neutropenia, rhabdomyolysis, fatigue, nausea/vomiting.
Imatinib/sunitinib/sorafenib/regorafenib: diarrhea, rash, hypertension, cardiac toxicity, hypothyroidism.
Larotrectinib/entrectinib/repotrectinib: fatigue, dizziness, weight gain, increased transaminases.
Sirolimus/everolimus/temsirolimus: stomatitis, rash, metabolic abnormalities, immunosuppression.
Pexidartinib: hepatotoxicity, edema, dysgeusia, fatigue; requires risk evaluation and mitigation strategy (REMS).
Vimseltinib: periorbital edema, fatigue, nausea, elevated creatine kinase.
surgery: Wound healing complications (especially after preoperative RT for lower extremity tumors: 43% vs 5% for upper extremity) [O'Sullivan 2002].
Nerve injury, vascular injury, loss of range of motion.
Need for complex reconstruction (plastic surgery involvement often required).
Prehabilitation and rehabilitation are strongly recommended to optimize functional outcomes [SARC-D 3 of 4].
radiation: Acute: dermatitis, fatigue, wound healing delay (higher with preoperative RT).
Late: fibrosis, edema, joint stiffness, secondary malignancy (rare), lymphoedema, bone fractures.
Postoperative RT fields are larger and doses higher (66 Gy vs 50 Gy), leading to increased late toxicity compared to preoperative RT [Davis 2005].
IMRT and image-guided RT can reduce late toxicity [Wang 2015].
Re-irradiation: increased risk of normal tissue injury; brachytherapy, IMRT, or proton therapy may reduce morbidity [EXTSARC-6].
Hypofractionated regimens for palliative/definitive RT: acceptable early local control, long-term data still maturing [SARC-E 4 of 6].
For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma, adjuvant RT is discouraged; neoadjuvant RT with IMRT is preferred to spare bowel [SARC-E 3 of 4].

supportive_care.overview: The NCCN Soft Tissue Sarcoma guidelines emphasize that all patients should be treated by a multidisciplinary team with expertise in sarcoma. Supportive care is integrated throughout the management pathway, including prehabilitation, rehabilitation, distress assessment, and management of treatment side effects. While the guideline does not provide exhaustive supportive care protocols, key elements are outlined in the Principles of Surgery (SARC-D) and by references to NCCN palliative care and distress management guidelines. Dexrazoxane is recommended as a cardioprotectant for patients receiving high cumulative doses of doxorubicin (>250 mg/m²) [SARC-G 8 of 14]. The use of growth factors (G-CSF) is implied in dose-dense regimens (e.g., IFADIC given every 14 days with growth factor support) [Fakhrai 2010]. Pain management should be integrated early into the rehabilitation plan, especially for neuropathic pain and phantom limb sensation [SARC-D 4 of 4]. Psychosocial support, including peer support groups, is recommended for all patients.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Prehabilitation for retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma should include nutritional supplementation as part of multimodality prehabilitation (exercise, nutrition, anxiety-coping) to improve functional capacity and reduce postoperative complications [SARC-D 3 of 4]. Specific nutritional guidelines are not provided but are considered part of standard oncology supportive care.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: The guideline does not specify antiemetic protocols; however, since anthracycline and ifosfamide-based regimens are highly emetogenic, standard NCCN antiemesis guidelines should be applied. The use of mesna for ifosfamide is recommended to prevent hemorrhagic cystitis [SARC-G 8 of 14].
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Growth factor support (filgrastim, lenograstim) is used in dose-intensive regimens such as the IFADIC regimen every 14 days [Fakhrai 2010] and in the MAID chemoradiation protocol [Kraybill 2006]. No specific guidance on primary prophylaxis is given, but it is implied for regimens with high febrile neutropenia risk.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: No specific VTE prophylaxis recommendations are provided in the STS guideline. Standard oncology VTE risk assessment and prophylaxis per NCCN guidelines should be applied, particularly for patients undergoing major surgery or receiving chemotherapy.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain management should be integrated into the rehabilitation plan early, especially for neuropathic pain or phantom limb sensation after amputation. Interventions can include mirror therapy, motor imagery, massage, oral and topical analgesics, coping strategies, and patient education [SARC-D 4 of 4]. For painful musculoskeletal metastases, ablation (including cement augmentation) can be used for pain palliation [SARC-F 2 of 3].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: All patients should be assessed for distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [EXTSARC-1]. Peer support groups should be offered. Referral to appropriate mental health professionals is recommended for body image issues and distress related to surgery/decreased physical ability [SARC-D 4 of 4].
supportive_care.dental_care: No specific dental care recommendations are provided in the guideline. Since some chemotherapy agents (e.g., ifosfamide, doxorubicin) can cause mucositis and immunosuppression, standard oral care protocols are advisable.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Post-treatment follow-up is risk-stratified by disease stage and site. For extremity/body wall/head/neck STS, the schedule is as follows: Stage I – H&P every 3–6 months for 3 years, then annually. Baseline and periodic imaging of the primary site based on risk of locoregional recurrence. Chest imaging (CT without contrast preferred or CXR) every 6–12 months [EXTSARC-2, SARC-A]. For Stage II/III and select Stage IV (any T, N1, M0) – H&P and imaging every 3–6 months for 3 years, then every 6 months to complete 5 years, then annually. Chest imaging and imaging of other known metastatic sites should be performed every 2–6 months for 2–3 years, then every 6 months to 5 years, then annually. Postoperative baseline and periodic imaging of the primary site are recommended [SARC-A 4 of 5]. For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal STS – physical exam with imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis preferred or MRI) every 3–6 months for 3 years, then every 6 months to complete 5 years, then annually [RETSARC-3, SARC-A 4 of 5]. For desmoid tumors – imaging every 3–6 months for 3 years, then every 6–12 months thereafter (MRI preferred; US may be considered for select locations) [SARC-A 5 of 5]. For ALT/WDLPS of extremity/abdominal wall/trunk – H&P every 6–12 months for 2 years, then annually. Imaging of primary site based on location and risk. Routine chest imaging is not necessary [ALT/WDLPS-1]. For borderline/malignant phyllodes – H&P and imaging (CT C/A/P and breast/chest MRI with contrast) every 3–6 months for 3 years, then every 6 months to 5 years, then annually. Symptom-driven brain MRI considered for 5 years for malignant phyllodes [PHYLLSARC-3, PHYLLSARC-4A].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The primary goal of surveillance is early detection of potentially curable recurrences, given that isolated local recurrences and oligometastatic (especially pulmonary) disease can be treated with salvage surgery or SBRT. Higher-grade/larger tumors have a higher risk of dissemination; internal hemorrhage/capsular rupture may also increase risk. Rigorous surveillance is most important in the first 3–5 years post-resection, as the majority of recurrences occur during this period. After 10 years, the likelihood of recurrence is small and follow-up should be individualized [EXTSARC-3A, SARC-A]. For desmoid tumors, surveillance focuses on local disease, as they do not metastasize. For WDLPS, chest imaging is not routinely needed due to low metastatic potential.
late_effects_screening: Late radiation toxicity: fibrosis, edema, joint stiffness, lymphedema, lymphangitis, secondary malignancy (sarcomas, skin cancers).
Cardiotoxicity from anthracyclines: long-term cardiac monitoring (echocardiogram) recommended for patients who received cumulative doxorubicin >250 mg/m².
Ifosfamide-related: nephrotoxicity (Fanconi syndrome), hemorrhagic cystitis, gonadal toxicity.
Neuropathy (from chemotherapy: vinca alkaloids, taxanes, platinum agents).
Functional disability after limb-sparing or amputation surgery: need for ongoing rehabilitation, prosthetics, and physical medicine and rehabilitation follow-up.
Psychological late effects: body image issues, anxiety, depression; offer psychosocial support and peer groups.
For patients who received radiation to the chest/abdomen: increased risk of cardiovascular disease, pulmonary fibrosis, secondary malignancies; consider age-appropriate cancer screening.
For patients with Li-Fraumeni or other genetic syndromes: tailored surveillance for multiple primary cancers.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: STS most commonly metastasizes to the lungs; retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas more frequently metastasize to liver and peritoneum. Some histologies have unique patterns: myxoid/round cell liposarcoma to spine and soft tissues; alveolar soft part sarcoma to brain; angiosarcoma to lymph nodes and CNS; leiomyosarcoma to abdomen/pelvis and bone; rhabdomyosarcoma to lymph nodes and bone. Local recurrence rates for extremity STS with negative margins and appropriate RT are approximately 10-15% at 10 years. For retroperitoneal STS, local recurrence remains a significant problem even after macroscopically complete resection. Desmoid tumors have high local recurrence rates (up to 30-50% after surgery alone) but have negligible metastatic potential. Recurrence patterns guide surveillance imaging: chest CT, primary site MRI, and site-specific imaging based on histology [SARC-A; EXTSARC-6; RETSARC-3].

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: H&P every 3–6 mo for 3 y, then annually
clinical_follow_up_schedule.notes: In areas easily followed by PE, imaging may not be required.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.disease_state: Extremity/body wall/head/neck Stage II/III and select IV
clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: H&P and imaging every 3–6 mo for 3 y, then every 6 mo to complete 5 y, then annually
clinical_follow_up_schedule.notes: More intensive for first 3-5 years due to higher risk of dissemination.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: Physical exam with imaging every 3–6 mo for 3 y, then every 6 mo to complete 5 y, then annually
clinical_follow_up_schedule.notes: For resected disease (RETSARC-3); for unresectable/stage IV, individualize.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: Imaging every 3–6 mo for 3 y, then every 6–12 mo thereafter
clinical_follow_up_schedule.notes: Initial imaging every 3 mo recommended; more frequent if symptomatic.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: H&P every 6–12 mo for 2 y, then annually
clinical_follow_up_schedule.notes: Imaging of primary site based on risk; routine chest imaging not needed.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: H&P and imaging (CT C/A/P and breast/chest MRI with/without contrast) every 3–6 mo for 3 y, then every 6 mo to 5 y, then annually
clinical_follow_up_schedule.notes: Consider symptom-driven brain MRI for 5 y for malignant phyllodes.

imaging_strategy.preferred: MRI with and without contrast (for extremity/body wall/head/neck/cardiac)
imaging_strategy.notes: US may be used for early detection of local recurrence (<0.5 cm nodules) or to differentiate solid nodule vs seroma/hematoma, but should be performed by experienced ultrasonographer. For long-term survivors, nonionizing modalities preferred.

imaging_strategy.schedule: Stage I: every 6–12 mo; Stage II/III/synchronous IV: every 2–6 mo for 2–3 y, then every 6 mo to 5 y, then annually.

imaging_strategy.indications: Alveolar soft part sarcoma, angiosarcoma, cardiac sarcoma (left-sided), malignant phyllodes (symptom-driven)
imaging_strategy.preferred: MRI brain with and without contrast

imaging_strategy.indications: Angiosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma, myxoid/round cell liposarcoma, retroperitoneal STS (on surveillance)

imaging_strategy.notes: Due to higher risk of spine metastases.
PET/CT may not identify extrapulmonary disease.

imaging_strategy.indications: Selective: for initial staging, prognostication, grading, response to neoadjuvant therapy; for differentiating WDLPS vs dedifferentiated liposarcoma; for high-grade tumors where metastatic spread is uncertain; for RMS staging due to nodal metastases and unusual sites
imaging_strategy.notes: FDG-PET/MRI acceptable if available. Consider for restaging after neoadjuvant therapy (change in ≥40% SUVmax associated with improved recurrence-free survival [Schuetze 2005]).

imaging_strategy.indications: Angiosarcoma, clear cell sarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma, cardiac sarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma
imaging_strategy.preferred: CT or FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI)

surveillance.laboratory_monitoring: No specific laboratory monitoring is outlined in the guideline beyond routine clinical care for chemotherapy toxicity and disease assessment. For patients on systemic therapy, complete blood counts, renal and hepatic function, and cardiac function (ECHO for anthracyclines) should be monitored as per standard of care and package inserts of individual agents.
supportive_follow_up: Assessment for rehabilitation needs: all patients who have undergone limb-sparing or amputative surgery should be offered PM&R consultation, prehabilitation (if time permits), and ongoing rehabilitation [SARC-D 3 of 4].
Distress screening: use NCCN Distress Thermometer at follow-up visits [EXTSARC-1].

Genetic counseling referral: for patients with Li-Fraumeni syndrome, neurofibromatosis, Lynch syndrome, FAP/Gardner syndrome, hereditary retinoblastoma, Carney-Stratakis syndrome, or personal/family history suggestive of other cancer predisposition syndromes [SARC-H, EXTSARC-1].
Long-term survivor care: after 5–10 years, follow-up should be individualized; chest x-rays and nonionizing imaging may be used to minimize radiation exposure [SARC-A].
Psychosocial support and peer support groups should be offered.

special_situations.situation: Previous unplanned excision of extremity/body wall/head/neck sarcoma
special_situations.key_message: Patients who present after unplanned excision (e.g., excision of a presumed benign mass that proves to be sarcoma) require staging imaging and careful management.
special_situations.management: If gross residual tumor: follow algorithms for primary treatment (EXTSARC-1). If no gross residual tumor but close/microscopically positive margins: manage as for failure to obtain oncologically appropriate margins (SARC-D 2 of 4). Re-resection is strongly recommended if feasible with minimal adverse functional impact. Following unplanned excision, there is increased risk of local recurrence, so combined local control approach with re-resection and RT (preferably preoperative) is supported. For high-risk stage III, consider systemic therapy.
special_situations.evidence: Data suggest increased risk of local recurrence after unplanned excision, supporting aggressive local control [SARC-D 1 of 4].

special_situations.situation: Failure to obtain oncologically appropriate margins (extremity/truncal/head/neck)
special_situations.key_message: Margins may be positive on critical structures (bone, nerve, major vessels) where re-resection would cause significant morbidity.
special_situations.management: If soft tissue margins are unexpectedly positive, surgical re-resection to negative margins should be strongly considered if technically possible with minimal adverse functional impact. For close/microscopically positive soft tissue margins that cannot be re-resected, adjuvant RT should be considered based on risk of future local recurrence and morbidity. For ALT/WDLPS, observation is recommended for focally positive margins if re-resection would not be unduly morbid. For some stage IA histologies, close observation is an option. For positive margins on critical structures (bone, nerve, major vessels), strong consideration for adjuvant RT (unless preoperative RT already given).
special_situations.evidence: Based on risk of local recurrence; RT boost for positive margins after preoperative RT does not clearly improve local control [Yami 2010; Pan 2014].

special_situations.situation: Fungating, necrotic, bleeding, and/or super-infected tumors
special_situations.key_message: These tumors may not allow for preoperative radiation and/or neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
special_situations.management: Better served with surgery followed by adjuvant therapy [EXTSARC-3A].

special_situations.situation: Retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma with R1 or R2 resection
special_situations.key_message: Adjuvant RT should not be administered routinely; exceptions are highly selected patients where local recurrence would cause undue morbidity.
special_situations.management: For R1 margins: Adjuvant RT not routinely recommended; consider re-resection if safe and feasible. For R2 margins: re-resection if biology, technical feasibility, and comorbidities allow; otherwise see primary treatment for unresectable disease. Consider adjuvant systemic therapy if high risk for metastatic disease (not for low-grade WDLPS).
special_situations.evidence: Given the proximity of critical structures, close/positive margins are common; data do not support routine adjuvant RT [RETSARC-3; Bonvalot 2020 STRASS trial showed no benefit for neoadjuvant RT in overall population].

special_situations.situation: Desmoid tumor in patients with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) or Gardner syndrome
special_situations.key_message: Desmoid tumors occur in 7.5–16% of FAP patients; relative risk is much higher than general population.
special_situations.management: Consider genetic counseling and testing for FAP. Management follows desmoid algorithms (DESM-1 to DESM-4). Systemic therapy options include NSAIDs (sulindac, celecoxib), hormonal therapy (tamoxifen, toremifene), tyrosine kinase inhibitors (imatinib, sorafenib), and cytotoxic chemotherapy. Surgery may be considered but has high recurrence rates. Observation is a first-line option.
special_situations.evidence: Risk factors: positive family history, abdominal surgery, APC mutation site [Nieuwenhuis 2011]. Spontaneous regression in up to 20% [Gounder 2023].

special_situations.situation: Rhabdomyosarcoma in adults: non-pleomorphic subtypes (alveolar, embryonal, spindle cell/sclerosing)
special_situations.key_message: RMS in adults is rare and has a worse prognosis than in children.
special_situations.management: Referral to institutions with RMS expertise is strongly recommended. Multimodality treatment (surgery, RT, chemotherapy) with pediatric-inspired multidisciplinary planning is required. Systemic therapy differs from other STS histologies (see SARC-G 2 of 14). Chemotherapy options include VAC, VAI-Europe, VDC-IE, and others. For intermediate-risk disease, maintenance chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide/vinorelbine may be considered.
special_situations.evidence: Adult RMS: 5-year OS 27–40% [Ferrari 2003; Sultan 2009]. Response to chemotherapy is favorable and correlates with survival [Esnaola 2001].

special_situations.key_message: Pleomorphic RMS is distinct from anaplastic variant in children and should be treated like high-grade soft tissue sarcoma.
special_situations.management: Treat according to algorithms for other soft tissue sarcomas of extremity/body wall/head/neck (EXTSARC-1). Use systemic therapy options from SARC-G 1 of 14 (not the RMS-specific options).
special_situations.evidence: Pleomorphic RMS is rare in children; histology is high-grade and behaves like UPS [RMS-1].

special_situations.situation: Atypical lipomatous tumor/well-differentiated liposarcoma (ALT/WDLPS) of extremity/trunk/abdominal wall
special_situations.key_message: ALT/WDLPS is generally indolent and has low metastatic potential.
special_situations.management: Surgical resection with oncologically appropriate margins; en bloc resection with negative margins is generally sufficient. RT is reserved for selected patients with recurrent or deeply infiltrative primary lesions. For focally positive margins, observation is recommended if re-resection in the event of recurrence would not be unduly morbid.
special_situations.evidence: ALT/WDLPS has no metastatic potential unless dedifferentiated. No survival benefit from RT in most cases [ALT/WDLPS-1].

special_situations.situation: Borderline phyllodes tumor of the breast
special_situations.key_message: Phyllodes tumors are rare fibroepithelial neoplasms with potential for local recurrence and, for malignant types, distant metastasis.
special_situations.management: Surgery to obtain negative margins (initial intent for 1 cm margin). Re-excision for positive margins (RT is not a substitute for negative margins). Adjuvant RT can be considered for increased risk of local recurrence (mitoses >30/10 HPF, size >10 cm, malignant heterologous elements). Genetic counseling and testing for breast and sarcoma genes recommended.
special_situations.evidence: Narrow surgical margins increase local recurrence risk. For borderline phyllodes, mastectomy not always needed if partial mastectomy fails 1 cm margin [PHYLLSARC-1].

special_situations.situation: Malignant phyllodes tumor of the breast
special_situations.key_message: Malignant phyllodes can metastasize (lung, bone, viscera) and requires aggressive local and systemic management.
special_situations.management: Surgical resection with 1 cm margin (mastectomy preferred to reduce local recurrence). Positive margins require additional surgical resection. Adjuvant RT considered after both breast conservation and mastectomy in high-risk scenarios. Consider adjuvant systemic therapy for high-risk features (mitoses >30/10 HPF, size >10 cm, malignant heterologous elements). For synchronous stage IV, manage per sarcoma guidelines with metastasectomy, SBRT, ablation, or systemic therapy.
special_situations.evidence: Local recurrence less likely with mastectomy; metastasectomy independently associated with OS (HR 0.33, 95% CI 0.14-0.78) [Neron 2020]. Follow-up includes symptom-driven brain MRI for 5 years [PHYLLSARC-2, PHYLLSARC-4A].

special_situations.situation: Isolated limb perfusion/infusion for unresectable extremity STS
special_situations.key_message: Regional limb therapy can be used as a limb-sparing approach for locally advanced disease not amenable to standard resection.
special_situations.management: Should only be performed at institutions with experience in the technique. For isolated regional disease or nodes, ILP/ILI may be accompanied by surgical resection.
special_situations.evidence: Meta-analyses suggest efficacy, but prospective randomized data are lacking [SARC-G; EXTSARC-4].

special_situations.situation: Primary sarcoma with synchronous regional nodal metastatic disease
special_situations.key_message: Management of the primary tumor follows stage-appropriate algorithms; management of nodal disease involves regional node dissection.
special_situations.management: Treat primary tumor as per EXTSARC-3/EXTSARC-4. For nodal involvement: resection of involved lymph nodes/regional nodal dissection ± RT ± systemic therapy [EXTSARC-6].
special_situations.evidence: Lymph node evaluation implications from NCDB data [Ecker 2017]; radical lymphadenectomy associated with improved survival [Fong 1993].

clinical_pearls: Multidisciplinary management by a sarcoma expert team is mandatory before initiating any treatment; adherence to NCCN guidelines is associated with improved survival.

Preoperative RT is preferred over postoperative RT for extremity STS due to lower total dose (50 Gy vs 66 Gy), smaller fields, and less late toxicity, but carries a higher risk of acute wound complications, especially in lower extremity tumors.

Surgery alone (with close observation) is reasonable for stage IA tumors resected with wide negative margins >1 cm or intact fascial plane; RT can be omitted for small (<5 cm), low-grade, superficial lesions.
For desmoid tumors, the first-line approach is often observation ('wait and see') due to spontaneous regression in up to 20% of patients; progression, not just presence, should trigger intervention.

Pembrolizumab plus RT followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab is now a recommended neoadjuvant/adjuvant regimen for UPS-related sarcomas (limb girdle/extremity UPS, myxofibrosarcoma, dedifferentiated liposarcoma, pleomorphic liposarcoma) based on SU2C-SARC032.

NTRK gene fusion-positive sarcomas should receive targeted therapy (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib) regardless of histologic subtype; these are effective even in heavily pretreated patients.

For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas, neoadjuvant RT can be considered in selected high-risk patients (especially liposarcoma), but the STRASS trial did not meet its primary endpoint; adjuvant RT is discouraged.

Biopsy technique is critical: core needle biopsy is preferred, placed along the future resection axis with minimal dissection and hemostasis; do not use fine-needle aspiration as the sole diagnostic method.

FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) is useful for staging high-risk sarcomas, assessing response to neoadjuvant therapy (≥40% reduction in SUVmax correlates with better outcomes), and distinguishing WDLPS from dedifferentiated liposarcoma.

Metastasectomy remains the historical standard for oligometastatic pulmonary disease and can be associated with long-term survival; however, SBRT, ablation, and embolization are acceptable alternatives.

For phyllodes tumors, surgical margin ≥1 cm is critical; mastectomy is often needed for malignant phyllodes to reduce local recurrence. Adjuvant RT should be considered for high-risk features (mitoses >30/10 HPF, size >10 cm, malignant heterologous elements).

In rhabdomyosarcoma, adult patients should be managed with pediatric-inspired multidisciplinary protocols; systemic therapy for non-pleomorphic RMS differs from standard STS - use VAC, VAI, or VDC-IE regimens.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Soft Tissue Sarcoma (Version 4.2026)
guidelines_resources.notes: Covers all soft tissue sarcoma subtypes including extremity/body wall/head/neck, retroperitoneal, desmoid tumors, rhabdomyosarcoma, atypical lipomatous tumor/WDLPS, and phyllodes tumors. Provides principles of imaging, pathology, surgery, radiation therapy, interventional oncology, systemic therapy, and genetic risk assessment.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017)
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides TNM and histologic grade-based staging for STS of trunk/extremity, retroperitoneum, head/neck, and abdomen/thoracic visceral organs. Uses FNCLCC grading system.

guidelines_resources.title: WHO Classification of Tumours of Soft Tissue and Bone, 5th Edition (2020)
guidelines_resources.notes: Standardized nomenclature for histologic subtypes of soft tissue tumors.

guidelines_resources.title: Protocol for the Examination of Specimens from Patients with Soft Tissue Tumors (2017)
guidelines_resources.notes: Standardized pathology reporting including margin status, grade, size, necrosis, mitotic rate, ancillary studies.

guidelines_resources.title: Radiation Therapy for Treatment of Soft Tissue Sarcoma in Adults: Executive Summary of an ASTRO Clinical Practice Guideline
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced in NCCN for RT principles [Salerno 2021; Pract Radiat Oncol 2021;11:339-351].

guidelines_resources.title: Management of Primary Retroperitoneal Sarcoma (RPS) in the Adult: A Consensus Approach
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides consensus on surgical and RT management for retroperitoneal sarcoma [Trans-Atlantic RPS Working Group 2015].

key_trials.population: Localized resectable soft-tissue sarcoma, meta-analysis of 14 randomized trials
key_trials.key_result: Doxorubicin-based chemotherapy prolonged local and distant recurrence and overall relapse-free survival (HR not reported). OS benefit not significant but trend in favor of chemotherapy (absolute OS benefit ~4% at 5 years not statistically significant).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: None reported beyond recurrence and OS
key_trials.practice_change: Established that adjuvant chemotherapy can reduce recurrence but survival benefit remains uncertain.

key_trials.full_name: European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer trial 62931
key_trials.population: Macroscopically resected grade II-III sarcoma, no metastases
key_trials.key_result: 5-year RFS 52% in both arms; OS 64% vs 69% (no significant difference).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No survival advantage for adjuvant chemotherapy
key_trials.practice_change: Confirmed that routine adjuvant chemotherapy is not indicated for all high-grade STS; selection for chemotherapy should be individualized.

key_trials.full_name: Italian randomized cooperative trial of adjuvant chemotherapy
key_trials.population: Patients with high-grade or recurrent extremity sarcoma
key_trials.key_result: Median DFS 48 vs 16 months (p=0.04); median OS 75 vs 46 months (p=0.04). 5-year OS 66% vs 46% (p=0.04).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Absolute OS benefit 13% at 2 years, 19% at 4 years.
key_trials.practice_change: Supports use of adjuvant anthracycline/ifosfamide in high-risk extremity STS.

key_trials.population: Stage III soft tissue sarcoma of extremity (including UPS, MFS, ddLPS, pleomorphic LPS)
key_trials.intervention: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab + RT followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab + surgery
key_trials.key_result: Improved event-free survival (results not presented in detail in NCCN text; cited as a positive trial leading to new recommendation)
key_trials.practice_change: Added as preferred neoadjuvant/adjuvant regimen for UPS-related sarcomas (limb girdle/extremity UPS/MFS/ddLPS/pleomorphic liposarcoma) [SARC-G 1 of 14].

key_trials.population: Metastatic non-lipogenic STS after failure of at least one anthracycline-based regimen
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 4.6 months vs 1.6 months (HR not explicitly given, p<0.0001). OS 12.5 vs 11 months (p=0.25, not significant).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Health-related quality of life did not improve or decline.
key_trials.practice_change: Pazopanib is a standard option for advanced/metastatic non-lipogenic STS after anthracycline failure.

key_trials.full_name: Eribulin versus dacarbazine in previously treated advanced liposarcoma or leiomyosarcoma
key_trials.population: Advanced liposarcoma (LPS) or leiomyosarcoma (LMS) after prior anthracycline-based therapy
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 13.5 vs 11.5 months; HR 0.77 (95% CI 0.62–0.95, p=0.017). Benefit was limited to LPS subgroup.
key_trials.practice_change: Eribulin is a category 1 option for advanced liposarcoma and category 2A for other STS subtypes.

key_trials.full_name: Efficacy and safety of trabectedin or dacarbazine for metastatic liposarcoma or leiomyosarcoma after failure of conventional chemotherapy
key_trials.population: Metastatic LPS or LMS after prior anthracycline-based therapy
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 4.2 vs 1.5 months (HR 0.55; 95% CI not given, p<0.0001). OS not significantly different (HR for death not reported).
key_trials.practice_change: Trabectedin is a category 1 recommendation for LPS and LMS, and category 2A for other STS subtypes.

key_trials.full_name: Nirogacestat, a gamma-secretase inhibitor for desmoid tumors
key_trials.key_result: Nirogacestat improved PFS per investigator; results reported as positive (category 1 recommendation).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Spontaneous regression in up to 20% of observation arm.
key_trials.practice_change: Nirogacestat added as preferred systemic therapy for desmoid tumors (category 1).

key_trials.full_name: Sorafenib for advanced and refractory desmoid tumor
key_trials.key_result: Objective response rate and PFS benefit; results led to category 1 recommendation.
key_trials.practice_change: Sorafenib is a preferred systemic therapy for desmoid tumors (category 1).

key_trials.full_name: Preoperative radiotherapy plus surgery versus surgery alone for patients with primary retroperitoneal sarcoma (EORTC-62092)
key_trials.key_result: Median ARFS 4.5 vs 5.0 years; HR 1.01 (p=0.95). No significant difference. Exploratory analysis suggested benefit in liposarcoma subgroup.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Common grade 3-4 adverse events: lymphopenia (77%), anemia (12%), hypoalbuminemia (12%) in RT+surgery group.
key_trials.practice_change: Neoadjuvant RT should not be considered standard of care for all retroperitoneal STS; may be considered in selected high-risk patients (e.g., liposarcoma).

key_trials.full_name: Pexidartinib versus placebo for advanced tenosynovial giant cell tumor (ENLIVEN)
key_trials.population: Advanced tenosynovial giant cell tumor (TGCT) not amenable to surgery
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Improvement in range of motion, pain, and quality of life.
key_trials.practice_change: Pexidartinib is category 1 recommendation for TGCT/PVNS; requires REMS due to hepatotoxicity.

key_trials.full_name: Vimseltinib versus placebo for tenosynovial giant cell tumour (MOTION)
key_trials.population: Advanced TGCT not amenable to surgery
key_trials.key_result: Significantly improved ORR; results led to category 1 recommendation.
key_trials.practice_change: Vimseltinib is a preferred systemic therapy for TGCT.
